1:19:58 they would stack up besides each other
1:20:00 like this. So kind of like in a line
1:20:02 horizontally. However, had he used
1:20:05 display block on his images, then they
1:20:07 would stack up on top of each other. So
1:20:10 rule of thumb is display block stacks on
1:20:13 top. Another way to look at this is that
1:20:15 the block level element blocks out the
1:20:18 rest of the available horizontal space
1:20:20 so that nothing else can be placed
1:20:22 there. And that is regardless of how
1:20:23 wide this image is, the content itself,
1:20:26 display block requires control over all
1:20:28 the horizontal space in its given
1:20:30 container. So when this block image is
1:20:33 placed here and you add a new image, it
1:20:35 would have to be placed underneath the
1:20:37 previous one. And if it is display block
1:20:39 as well, it'll block out the rest of the
1:20:41 available horizontal space and the next
1:20:43 image have to be placed below that one
1:20:45 again and so on. So the solution is
1:20:48 obviously to turn these elements into

1:36:34 than we have available. And setting the
1:36:37 width of it is actually an important
1:36:38 thing in order to center it because
1:36:40 there are three things that need to be
1:36:43 in place when we are to center elements
1:36:46 with the technique you are about to
1:36:47 learn aka centering with margins. And
1:36:50 these three components are first the
1:36:52 element needs to be display blocked
1:36:54 because how is an element supposed to
1:36:56 center itself if it doesn't command full
1:36:59 control over all the horizontal space.
1:37:02 If it's display inline and allows other
1:37:04 elements to stack themselves besides the
1:37:07 element, well then it won't be able to
1:37:09 center itself because that space now is
1:37:11 busy with other things. So in order to
1:37:14 center this poo, it needs to control all
1:37:17 the horizontal space.
1:37:19 So it needs this
1:37:22 block like that. Now that didn't change
1:37:25 anything, but under the hood, the poo
1:37:27 now controls the full horizontal space.
1:37:30 Okay, the second part is that it must
1:37:32 have a width
1:37:34 and that is what we've done here already
1:37:37 because how is this item supposed to be
1:37:40 centered inside the browser if it is
1:37:41 wider than the browser itself? That's
1:37:44 often what happens with either large
1:37:46 elements or with some HTML elements who
1:37:48 just naturally actually expand to take
1:37:51 up all the available space in the block.
1:37:53 So we have to make sure that our element
1:37:55 has a width. And finally we set the
1:37:58 margin on the left hand side and the
1:38:00 right hand side to the value auto. Let's
1:38:03 just do that. Margin left auto. Oh wow.
1:38:09 Now it pushed the pool all the way to
1:38:12 the right hand side because auto here
1:38:14 means take up all the available space.
1:38:17 And since this is a block element, the
1:38:20 available space is all the space that's
1:38:22 left after the pooh has taken up its 200
1:38:25 pixels. So it just ate up all of the
1:38:27 space and turned it into margin.
1:38:30 However, at this point, margin right
1:38:32 enters the party. Margin right and says
1:38:35 I want the available space as well. And
1:38:37 at that point, the margin left and
1:38:39 margin right goes into discussion and
1:38:41 decides, well, actually, since there are
1:38:43 two of us here pushing on each of the
1:38:45 side of the element, trying to get all
1:38:47 of the available white space, let's just
1:38:49 divide it evenly amongst both of us. And
1:38:52 the result, well, it is that the item is
1:38:55 centered. So, that was a long
1:38:57 explanation of centering with margins.

2:31:28 Okay, now we're going to do a little bit
2:31:30 of housekeeping here because this image
2:31:33 element actually has a serious flaw
2:31:36 because it lacks an important ingredient
2:31:38 that all image tags should have which is
2:31:41 called the alt text or the alternative
2:31:44 text and that is simply an attribute
2:31:47 that is written like this alt equals and
2:31:50 then inside of quotes you are to write a
2:31:53 description of the image. So here it
2:31:56 could be for example Pier Borggan
2:31:58 smiling like that. However, if we run
2:32:01 this code now, you can see that
2:32:02 seemingly nothing has changed. So why do
2:32:05 we at all write these old texts? Well,
2:32:08 first of all, it makes your site
2:32:09 accessible because imagine that a person
2:32:12 who is visually impaired or who is blind
2:32:14 is trying to visit your website. In that
2:32:17 case, they would use a screen reader
2:32:19 which goes through the HTML and reads it
2:32:22 out loud to the user. And as for the H3
2:32:26 tag, it would be simple for the screen
2:32:27 reader to read out loud what that says
2:32:30 because that is text. However, for an
2:32:32 image like this one, you need to add an
2:32:35 alt tag to describe the image so that
2:32:37 the screen reader has something to read
2:32:39 out loud. And secondly, this also works
2:32:41 as a fallback if your image doesn't
2:32:43 render properly cuz let's say you have a
2:32:46 spelling mistake in the sorts attribute,
2:32:49 for example, an extra R. Well, in that
2:32:51 case, the image won't render, but the
2:32:53 alt tag provides as a backup so that the
2:32:56 user seeing this can kind of understand
2:32:58 what was at least supposed to be there.
2:33:00 And finally, writing alt text will help
2:33:03 your site rank higher on Google, meaning
2:33:05 you get more search traffic. And that is
2:33:08 because the alt tag most likely provide
2:33:10 important metadata about the image so
2:33:12 that Google knows how to use it in a
2:33:14 search results page. And if your site is
2:33:16 not accessible, it might be punished in
2:33:18 the form of ranking lower. So with that
2:33:21 in mind, let's look at how to write
2:33:22 these alt texts. And the first thing you
2:33:25 need to know is that you should not
2:33:26 include for example image of because
2:33:28 that's simply not necessary. The screen
2:33:30 reader knows that it is an image because
2:33:32 it is an image tag.
2:33:34 Secondly, should be concise meaning less
2:33:37 than 125 characters because a lot of
2:33:40 screen readers will cut off after this
2:33:42 limit has been reached. And finally, a
2:33:44 trick you should use in order to come up
2:33:45 with a good old text is imagine that you
2:33:48 are describing this image to a person
2:33:50 over the phone. So let's try to do that
2:33:53 with an example. Here we have an iconic
2:33:55 image of Muhammad Ali in a boxing match.
2:33:58 And one way to write an old text for
2:34:00 this could be just a boxing match, but
2:34:03 it would actually be a pretty poor way
2:34:05 of describing it because there's so much
2:34:06 more going on here. You have plenty more
2:34:08 characters you can use in order to
2:34:10 describe it. So, a better one would be
2:34:12 two boxers in the ring, one lying down
2:34:14 and the other standing up. That's a good
2:34:17 factual explanation, but we're not quite
2:34:19 there yet because it isn't actually
2:34:21 describing this iconic moment when
2:34:24 Muhammad Ali knocked out Sunonny Listen.
2:34:26 So, if you were to describe this over
2:34:28 the phone, you would probably understand
2:34:30 that this description is pretty shallow.

2:34:35 text for this image would be Muhammad
2:34:37 Ali in the boxing ring screaming at
2:34:39 Sunonni listen after knocking him out.
2:34:41 That in my opinion properly describes
2:34:44 the intent of this image. What the

2:54:07 of the front-end career path, you will
2:54:09 be taking another one of my courses,
2:54:10 either the APIs and asynchronous
2:54:12 JavaScript course or the React course
2:54:14 that's later on in the path. Okay, back
2:54:16 to the topic at hand. Let's learn about
2:54:18 inheritance and why it's important to
2:54:20 learn about inheritance in the first
2:54:22 place. First of all, it will help you
2:54:24 write, you could say, drier code. Dry
2:54:26 stands for don't repeat yourself. In
2:54:28 other words, you can take advantage of
2:54:30 this concept of CSS inheritance so that
2:54:32 you don't have to keep writing the same
2:54:34 CSS code over and over, but instead can
2:54:37 define it in one place and have that
2:54:38 change cascade throughout the rest of
2:54:41 your CSS. Inheritance is also something
2:54:43 that can help you avoid bugs in your
2:54:45 code and generally be more efficient. To
2:54:48 demonstrate this, we've pulled in an
2:54:50 article from Wikipedia here about the
2:54:52 dancing plague of 1518. This is a really
2:54:55 strange phenomenon that happened in
2:54:57 modern-day France where somewhere
2:54:59 between 50 and up to 400 people
2:55:02 inexplicably took to the streets and
2:55:03 started dancing as it would appear
2:55:05 against their will. It's a fascinating
2:55:07 story. Feel free to read through the
2:55:09 events. There's even an illustration
2:55:11 here. I have no idea what could cause
2:55:13 this, but there's some pretty good
2:55:14 theories here that include food
2:55:16 poisoning and so forth. So, feel free to
2:55:18 look through this article, but that's
2:55:19 just for fun. Let's not go too far down
2:55:21 this rabbit hole. Instead, let's talk
2:55:23 about inheritance in the context of this
2:55:25 article. Since we've pulled this into
2:55:27 our own page, maybe we've decided that
2:55:29 we would like to center all of the text
2:55:31 on this page. And so, I could go to my
2:55:33 styles.css and start centering each one
2:55:36 of these elements by itself. For
2:55:39 example, we have an H1, a paragraph, an
2:55:41 H2, more paragraphs. So, sure, I could
2:55:44 start by saying I want to select the H1
2:55:47 and do text align center. Okay, I've
2:55:50 centered my text there for the H1. Now,
2:55:53 let's go to our H2 and do the exact same
2:55:56 thing. So, we'll go ahead and center the
2:55:59 H2. Great. We're partway there. Then, we
2:56:01 have to also select the paragraphs. And
2:56:03 hopefully, you can see the point I'm
2:56:04 trying to make here. In this case, we
2:56:06 would not consider our code very dry
2:56:08 because we are repeating oursel instead
2:56:11 of following DRY. Don't repeat yourself.
2:56:14 So, what does this have to do with
2:56:15 inheritance? Well, let's look at our
2:56:17 HTML and we can see that our entire
2:56:19 article is conveniently placed inside of
2:56:22 an article element. The idea of an
2:56:24 article element in HTML is
2:56:26 self-contained content that if you were
2:56:28 to remove it and put it somewhere else,
2:56:30 it would still make sense. Of course,
2:56:32 the dancing plague of 1518 is hard to
2:56:34 make any sense of, but I think you get
2:56:36 my gist. In this case, if we really do
2:56:38 want everything in our article to be
2:56:40 text align center, we can take advantage
2:56:42 of CSS inheritance by instead of
2:56:45 repeating all of our rules here, we can
2:56:47 say everything inside of the article
2:56:50 should be text align center. And as you
2:56:52 can see, this single rule has been
2:56:54 inherited by all of its children
2:56:56 elements where text align center makes
2:56:58 sense. Now, I could have chosen to put
2:57:00 text align center up on the body
2:57:03 instead. And you can see we get the same
2:57:05 effect. Essentially, we can know that
2:57:07 this inheritance doesn't just happen
2:57:08 with the direct children of the element
2:57:11 that we're selecting in CSS, but will
2:57:13 work any number of layers down the
2:57:15 descendant tree of the element that
2:57:16 we're selecting. I personally prefer to
2:57:18 have it on the article because maybe
2:57:20 there's other elements that we don't
2:57:22 want to have text align center that are
2:57:24 outside of the article tag. So, let's
2:57:26 leave it here on our article. Now to
2:57:28 refine our understanding of inheritance,
2:57:30 it's important to know that text align
2:57:32 is an example of a CSS property that
2:57:34 does get inherited down the tree.
2:57:36 However, not all CSS properties will get
2:57:39 inherited. I have found that it's pretty
2:57:41 intuitive which ones will and which ones
2:57:42 won't. But like we should do anytime we
2:57:44 have a question as we're learning
2:57:45 something new, we can Google it. Here
2:57:47 you can see we've Googled which CSS
2:57:49 properties are inherited. And there's a
2:57:51 Stack Overflow question exactly about
2:57:52 this. If we click into it, we can see
2:57:55 down here somebody has answered it and
2:57:56 begun a list. If we scroll down, we get
2:57:58 all the way down to 41 different CSS
2:58:01 properties that are inherited. Now, let
2:58:03 me be honest, most of these are not CSS
2:58:06 properties that you're going to really
2:58:07 have to care too much about. In fact,

3:24:32 exactly that means, but it's important

3:24:38 machine didn't come with Git already

3:24:42 you would have to download and install

3:24:46 main task is to help us keep a history
3:24:48 of the changes that we've made to our
3:24:50 projects. In contrast, GitHub is an
3:24:53 online platform that stores something
3:24:55 called Git repositories. The term
3:24:58 repository is just kind of a fancy way
3:25:00 to say a folder of your code that you
3:25:02 have been tracking the changes of in
3:25:04 Git. In contrast with Git, which runs
3:25:07 locally on your own machine, GitHub runs
3:25:09 online in the cloud. And where Git's
3:25:12 main task is to keep track of a history
3:25:14 of the changes you've made to your
3:25:15 project, GitHub is a way for one to
3:25:17 share your code and that history of the
3:25:20 changes from Git with other people.
3:25:22 Maybe an imperfect but illustrative
3:25:24 analogy is Git is kind of like taking
3:25:27 photos on your phone or your camera,
3:25:29 which happens locally on your own

3:25:38 differences between Git and GitHub.
3:25:41 Okay, we talked about version control.
3:25:43 So, what exactly is version control? The
3:25:45 main thing that version control provides
3:25:47 for us is a way for us to create save
3:25:49 points, which in Git are called commits
3:25:51 of your code so that you can revert back
3:25:53 to them if needed. These save points or
3:25:55 commits aren't something that actually
3:25:57 alters the content of your code itself.
3:25:59 This information is saved in kind of a
3:26:01 separate hidden folder inside of your
3:26:03 project that is using git, but it
3:26:05 provides you a way that if let's say you
3:26:07 were to write some kind of bug into your
3:26:09 code or accidentally delete everything
3:26:11 and then close your editor that you
3:26:13 couldn't just undo those changes with a
3:26:15 git commit, we can revert back to that
3:26:17 commit and have everything restored the
3:26:19 way that it was at that save point. This
3:26:22 reminds me of the most difficult level
3:26:24 of any video game I've ever played in my
3:26:26 life. If you know, you know. And I
3:26:28 probably would have enjoyed this game a
3:26:30 lot more if I had the ability to create
3:26:32 safe points along the way that I could
3:26:33 go to every time I died or failed.
3:26:36 Another major benefit of version control
3:26:38 is that it manages changes made by other
3:26:40 people that are also working on the same
3:26:42 codebase by giving us a means to merge
3:26:44 multiple different versions of the same
3:26:46 codebase together and fix any of the
3:26:48 resulting conflicts. If you've ever
3:26:50 worked on a group paper and well, you're
3:26:53 old enough, you might be familiar with
3:26:55 this phenomenon. As different people are
3:26:57 working on their own parts of the paper,

3:27:03 unique names. And then you'd have to

3:27:08 different versions and merge them
3:27:09 together into one version that became
3:27:11 the final paper the group would turn in.
3:27:13 In the end, it just becomes a huge pain.
3:27:15 And this is something that version
3:27:17 control helps us mitigate. Now, this
3:27:19 isn't to say that you won't have two
3:27:21 people that make, let's say, a change to
3:27:23 the same line of code, but in different
3:27:25 ways, but rather git as a version
3:27:27 control system gives us a way to

3:27:31 one of those changes we want to include
3:27:33 in the final codebase. In the end,
3:27:35 version control generally allows us to

3:27:39 mistakes or having our code conflict

3:53:11 applied once in this entire document.

3:53:16 again and again. It's meant for reusage.

3:53:21 here that also said for example FAQ's

3:53:30 styling as this button. But if you did
3:53:32 that with IDs and turned the btn here

4:13:57 Before we wrap up this section, I want
4:13:59 to drop in and teach you about a small
4:14:01 yet important part of your HTML
4:14:03 document, and that is setting the
4:14:06 document language using the HTML lang
4:14:09 attribute. And this is important for
4:14:11 several reasons. First, screen readers
4:14:14 use the lang attribute to determine
4:14:16 which language to use when reading the
4:14:17 content. And this is essential for users
4:14:19 who rely on assisted technologies.
4:14:22 Search engines also use the lang
4:14:23 attribute to understand the language of
4:14:25 your content. And it also ensures
4:14:27 consistency in language specific
4:14:29 features like date formats and specific
4:14:31 character sets. Setting the language is
4:14:34 quite straightforward. In your HTML
4:14:37 file, you'll use the lang attribute like
4:14:39 this in your opening HTML tag. And the
4:14:42 value of this attribute should be a
4:14:44 standard language code. For example, to
4:14:46 set the language of your document to
4:14:48 English, you would use en. And for
4:14:51 Spanish, it would be E S for espanol.
4:14:54 These language codes are standardized
4:14:56 and usually consist of two or three
4:14:58 letters. You can easily find the correct
4:15:00 code for your language with a quick
4:15:01 online search. Sometimes your page might
4:15:03 contain content in multiple language. In
4:15:06 that case, you'll set the primary
4:15:07 language in the HTML tag and use the
4:15:10 lang attribute on elements with
4:15:11 different languages. For example, in the
4:15:13 H3 tag, add a lang attribute and set it
4:15:16 to Spanish. Setting the language of your
4:15:18 page might seem like a small step, but
4:15:21 it does play a big role in accessibility
4:15:23 and helps your website reach the right
4:15:25 audience. To learn more about the ling
4:15:28 attribute and all the different language

6:24:07 comma and actually finishes with this

6:25:03 working. So what exactly are these
6:25:05 escape characters doing? Well, what the
6:25:07 escape character does is it tells
6:25:09 JavaScript that the next character
6:25:11 should not be seen as a string
6:25:14 delimiter. So the next character is not
6:25:16 starting or finishing a string. It's
6:25:18 just part of the string. So whenever you
6:25:21 run into the problem of having to use a
6:25:23 quotation mark or an apostrophe inside a
6:25:26 string and you get an error because it's
6:25:28 clashing with your string delimiters,
6:25:30 i.e. whatever you're using to open and
6:25:33 close the string. Well, you can use the
6:25:35 escape character to tell JavaScript that
6:25:38 the character that comes next is not to
6:25:40 be treated as a string delimiter. It's
6:25:42 not to be treated as a piece of code.
6:25:44 It's just part of the string. Now, as
6:25:47 you spend more time in coding, you will
6:25:48 come across escape characters more and
6:25:50 more. But that is just a really quick

7:32:52 equals, is that it's a little less

7:33:09 the code, then it doesn't work. Now, it
7:33:11 says not alleible even though 100 is
7:33:13 here as a number and 100 is here as a

8:36:22 are zero index. So the first is zero,
8:36:24 the second is one, the third is two. So
8:36:27 it would be natural to believe that
8:36:28 thelength property of this feature posts
8:36:32 is two because the final index is two.
8:36:35 But whereas indexes are zero indexed,
8:36:39 the length is not zero indexed. We count
8:36:42 the length of an array in just the same
8:36:44 way you would count anything in life,
8:36:46 which is starting at the number one. So
8:36:49 that also means that the length property
8:36:51 of an array is always one larger than
8:36:54 the final index. So this is one of those
8:36:57 things that are a little bit unintuitive
8:36:58 and confusing when learning JavaScript
8:37:00 and you probably will mix it up a couple
8:37:02 times. That's not a problem. Just trust
8:37:05 me when I say that eventually this will
8:37:07 become second nature for you. So let's
8:37:10 move on.
8:37:14 Okay, up until now, our arrays have
8:37:17 either included only strings or only
8:37:20 numbers. However, they're not limited to
8:37:23 that. Actually, put any data type you
8:37:25 want into an array. And you can mix as
8:37:27 much as you want. So, you can think of
8:37:29 an arrays as kind of a composition of
8:37:32 multiple data types and their values.
8:37:34 So, this is why arrays are what we call
8:37:37 a composite data type. You can say it's
8:37:39 composed by its items if you needed to
8:37:41 make semantically sense. And you'll
8:37:44 often hear this also referred to as
8:37:46 complex data types. That is arrays and
8:37:48 also for example objects which you are
8:37:50 going to learn about later. They stand
8:37:52 in contrast to the primitive data types
8:37:55 you have worked with up until now. The
8:37:58 three primitives you have learned are
8:38:00 strings, numbers, and booleans because
8:38:03 they are more primitive than arrays.
8:38:05 They're not composed of many other data
8:38:07 types which arrays often are. So one
8:38:10 analogy you can use is that the
8:38:13 primitive data types are the Lego blocks
8:38:16 whereas the array the composite data
8:38:18 types is kind of like the full Lego
8:38:20 model. For example, after you've built a
8:38:22 little house with your Lego blocks. But
8:38:24 anyway, you shouldn't worry too much
8:38:26 about this if you find it a little bit
8:38:27 abstract and complex. As a developer,
8:38:30 you don't go around thinking about, oh,

11:36:45 necessary, which could make our

11:36:48 the long run. So, first I'll update the

14:05:01 command line? Well, in short, the
14:05:04 command line is a user interface that
14:05:07 allows us to utilize the functions of
14:05:09 our device. Depending on your operating
14:05:12 system, the command line tool can have
14:05:14 different names like the terminal on Mac
14:05:16 OS or the PowerShell on Windows. But
14:05:19 don't let you confuse by that.
14:05:20 Essentially, these really are just
14:05:22 different names for one and the same
14:05:24 tool. Here on Scribba, the command line
14:05:27 goes under the name terminal. And
14:05:29 throughout this course, I will therefore
14:05:31 use the word terminal to refer to our
14:05:33 command line. As the name suggests, the
14:05:36 command line is all about commands. We
14:05:39 type a certain command in the terminal
14:05:41 to perform a certain action in the
14:05:43 operating system. We can compare this to
14:05:46 the graphical user interface that every
14:05:48 single one of us use on a daily basis on
14:05:51 both our laptops and our smartphones.

14:07:31 the command line interface over the

14:27:04 Creating and deleting directories is
14:27:06 very similar to creating and deleting
14:27:08 files. The syntax is basically the same
14:27:11 and we have the exact same options for
14:27:13 using paths to perform the creation and
14:27:15 deletion somewhere else than in the
14:27:17 current working directory. Though there
14:27:19 is one very important difference and
14:27:21 that is that we need to use a different
14:27:22 set of commands. Let's start with a
14:27:25 quick recap here. As you know, we use
14:27:27 the command touch to create files and
14:27:30 the command rm to delete files. When
14:27:33 working with directories, we create new
14:27:35 ones with the command maked, which is
14:27:37 short for make directory. And then, just
14:27:39 as with files, we write out the name
14:27:41 that we want to give to our new
14:27:43 directory. To delete a directory, we use
14:27:45 the command rm dur, short for remove
14:27:48 directory. And again, we must of course
14:27:50 specify which directory we want to
14:27:52 delete by writing out its name. When it
14:27:55 comes to deleting, there is one more
14:27:57 very important thing that you need to
14:27:58 know and that is that the command rmder
14:28:01 only works if the directory that you
14:28:03 want to delete is empty. If you want to
14:28:06 delete a directory that is not empty,
14:28:08 you must use the command rm- r instead
14:28:12 of rmd dur. Trying to use rmdur on a
14:28:15 directory that is not empty does not
14:28:16 break anything. So don't worry about
14:28:18 that. It simply causes an error saying
14:28:20 something like the directory is not
14:28:22 empty. And that is when you know that
14:28:24 you should use RM-R instead. My uncle

14:32:02 In the next couple of scrims, we'll
14:32:04 learn how we can use the terminal to
14:32:06 write to and read from files. The most
14:32:09 basic thing we can do when it comes to
14:32:10 writing is to write a string in the
14:32:12 terminal that is an also printed in the
14:32:15 terminal right below where you typed it.
14:32:18 To do that, we use the command echo
14:32:20 followed by a string like hello world.
14:32:22 Here, what echo does is simply that it
14:32:25 echoes or repeats what you just said and
14:32:28 therefore the output of echo will always
14:32:30 be the same as your input. Let's quickly
14:32:33 jump back into the editor so that you
14:32:35 can try this out on your own. In this
14:32:37 mini challenge, I want you to write a
14:32:40 greeting to yourself in the terminal
14:32:42 following this pattern here. Hello, and
14:32:45 then your name and finally an
14:32:47 exclamation mark at the end. Make sure
14:32:49 to wrap the string in single quotes just
14:32:51 like I've done in the example. Okay,
14:32:54 give this one a go. All right, I'm sure
14:32:56 that was not a problem for you and I'm
14:32:58 not going to walk you through this one
14:33:00 actually as it was just a minute
14:33:01 challenge. Let's instead return to the
14:33:03 slides and continue learning about
14:33:05 writing to files. If we want to take the
14:33:08 string hello world and print it to a
14:33:10 file instead of to the terminal, what we
14:33:13 need to do is to redirect the output of
14:33:15 echo. We can do this with what we call
14:33:18 the redirection operator followed by the
14:33:21 name of the file that we want to write
14:33:23 to. The expression that you see here
14:33:26 echo hello world redirection operator
14:33:29 hello.txt will result in the string
14:33:32 hello world being printed to the file
14:33:34 hello.txt.
14:33:36 The first part of the expression with
14:33:38 the command echo and the string hello
14:33:39 world is the exact same as before. But
14:33:42 now after the string, we add the
14:33:44 redirection operator and we then specify
14:33:47 the file where we want the string
14:33:48 printed. Finally, it's important to note
14:33:51 that echo can also create files for us.
14:33:54 Said that we run the same command as
14:33:56 before with redirection and everything,
14:33:59 but the file hello.txt does not yet
14:34:02 exist in our project. In this case, Echo
14:34:05 will create this new file for us. At the
14:34:08 time of recording though, there is an
14:34:10 issue in the Scribba IDE that causes
14:34:13 this specific syntax here where we use
14:34:15 echo to create the new file while
14:34:18 writing to it to not always work as
14:34:20 expected. So, just be aware that
14:34:22 currently you have to first create the
14:34:24 new file using touch and then write to
14:34:26 it using echo. But this is normally not
14:34:28 necessary. It's a temporary bug. when we
14:34:31 redirect an output, all existing content
14:34:34 in the file that we are redirecting to
14:34:36 will be overwritten. So instead of just
14:34:39 saying that this expression here, echo
14:34:41 hello world redirection operator
14:34:43 hello.txt, instead of saying that it
14:34:45 redirects the output of echo to
14:34:47 hello.txt, we could be more specific and
14:34:50 say that it redirects and prints our
14:34:52 output and at the same time overwrites
14:34:55 existing content.
14:34:57 To avoid overwriting and instead append
14:35:00 the output to the existing content, we
14:35:02 need to use a different operator, namely
14:35:04 the append redirection operator. So we
14:35:06 take the same character as before, the
14:35:09 arrow head pointing to the right, but we
14:35:11 make it a double this time instead of a
14:35:12 single like in this example here where
14:35:15 we say echo welcome back and then the
14:35:18 append redirection operator followed by
14:35:20 the file name. This results in the
14:35:22 string welcome back being printed below
14:35:25 hello world in the file hello.txt
14:35:28 because we use the append redirection
14:35:30 operator to append content instead of
14:35:32 overwriting.

14:47:02 today I'm going to be talking to you
14:47:03 about making branches and pull requests
14:47:06 with GitHub Desktop. Last time you
14:47:08 learned a little bit about setting up a
14:47:10 local dev environment using VS Code and
14:47:12 GitHub Desktop, VS Code for editing, and
14:47:15 GitHub Desktop to publish and sync your
14:47:17 local repositories with GitHub. Today,
14:47:19 I'd like to help you expand your
14:47:20 knowledge even further by learning the
14:47:22 basic workflow of how teams of
14:47:24 developers use GitHub to manage code. So
14:47:27 far, you've learned how to manage code
14:47:28 on a single branch, the main branch of
14:47:31 your codebase. You can think of a branch
14:47:33 as a copy or a version of your codebase.
14:47:36 And as your coding projects change and
14:47:39 get larger, GitHub can actually keep
14:47:41 track of many versions or branches. When
14:47:44 you're on a dev team, before you begin
14:47:46 the work to fix a bug or add a feature,
14:47:48 you typically create what is called a
14:47:50 feature branch. Different companies and
14:47:52 workplaces are going to have different
14:47:54 names for the feature branch. But the
14:47:56 basic idea is that you can work on
14:47:57 fixing bugs and adding features to your
14:47:59 code without affecting your main code
14:48:02 base at all. So you might be wondering
14:48:03 why do that? Why not just edit your main
14:48:05 codebase? Well, if you're working on a
14:48:07 small project, it may not be necessary.
14:48:09 But when you're working as part of a
14:48:11 team and as that team and your project
14:48:13 gets bigger and there are more people
14:48:15 working on it, more bugs may be
14:48:16 introduced, more conflicts might arise
14:48:19 in the code. and there's just a whole
14:48:21 lot more room for things to go wrong.
14:48:23 So, how do we better collaborate on
14:48:25 code? How do we keep track of the
14:48:27 changes that our team is making? The
14:48:29 answer to that is branching and pull
14:48:31 requests. A pull request is a
14:48:32 collaborative feature on GitHub that
14:48:34 gives teams a way to review each other's
14:48:35 changes before introducing the new code
14:48:38 back into the main branch. Here's a
14:48:40 simplified example of what this might
14:48:42 look like. So, a dev spins a feature
14:48:44 branch off the main branch, does some
14:48:46 work or fixes a bug, makes a pull
14:48:48 request, and then that pull request is
14:48:50 reviewed by a fellow team member or
14:48:52 multiple team members and is merged
14:48:55 eventually back into the main branch. In
14:48:58 the next few scrims, this is what we
14:48:59 will learn how to do. Make branches and
14:49:01 create PRs. You obviously won't be doing
14:49:03 this as part of a team, but you will
14:49:05 gain practice and familiarity with the
14:49:07 process for when you are working as part
14:49:08 of a team. So, join me in the next scrim

15:05:48 teacher here on Scribba. I wanted to
15:05:50 spend just a second and talk about SSH
15:05:52 and GPG keys in GitHub. Now, don't let
15:05:55 these acronyms scare you away. We're
15:05:56 going to keep it really light and at a
15:05:58 very high level here. Now, SSH and GPG
15:06:00 use something called public key
15:06:02 cryptography. And my goal isn't for us
15:06:04 to dive deep into public key
15:06:06 cryptography at this point, but instead
15:06:08 to give us a really highle overview of
15:06:09 the concept of public and private keys.
15:06:12 In the early days of computing and
15:06:13 especially dealing with the internet and
15:06:15 the web, the ability to securely pass
15:06:17 data from one source to another has
15:06:20 always been an issue on people's minds.
15:06:22 Public and private keys give us a method
15:06:24 for securely doing so. Not only that,
15:06:27 and perhaps an even more important
15:06:29 benefit of using public key cryptography
15:06:31 is that it provides a super high level
15:06:33 of confidence that the data being sent
15:06:35 is actually coming from the place that
15:06:37 it claims to be coming from. In terms of
15:06:40 dealing with GitHub, using an SSH key
15:06:42 gives us the ability to authenticate
15:06:44 with GitHub so that GitHub has
15:06:46 confidence that we are the real one
15:06:48 connecting with their system as we push
15:06:50 code up and pull code down.
15:06:52 Alternatively, we can use HTTPS, but
15:06:55 it's a little less convenient because we
15:06:57 have to provide our username and
15:06:58 password periodically to GitHub in order
15:07:00 to authenticate. Similarly, GPG keys
15:07:03 with GitHub allow us to verify that the
15:07:06 commits we're making to repositories on
15:07:08 GitHub are actually coming from us.
15:07:11 Again, both of these are ways that
15:07:12 GitHub is using to give a very high
15:07:14 level of confidence to GitHub that as we
15:07:17 are interacting with our accounts on
15:07:19 GitHub, it's the real us that's doing
15:07:20 so. That's about as deep as I'm going to

15:31:20 Let's take a look at object dstructuring
15:31:22 which is going to make your coding life
15:31:24 much easier. What exactly is it? Well,
15:31:27 object destructuring enables us to
15:31:29 extract properties from objects into
15:31:31 distinct variables. And if that doesn't
15:31:34 immediately make sense to you, don't
15:31:36 worry. Let's head straight into a
15:31:38 practical example. Can you recognize my

15:32:17 to have quite a bit more typing to do.
15:32:19 Let me just do it quickly. Okay, that
15:32:22 was boring and obnoxious to do and there
15:32:24 were only five properties in the object.
15:32:27 Imagine if there had been 50. But at
15:32:29 least now I can log out my sentence. So
15:32:31 if I hit save, we can see that down in
15:32:34 the console, we're getting the sentence.
15:32:37 So it works. But this is only a small
15:32:40 object. And we have written out the word

15:32:45 favorite film five times. and all of

15:33:00 there were 50 properties in here that we
15:33:02 wanted to use, that would take forever.

15:33:08 comment out all of this code. Now, I'm
15:33:11 going to come in here and I'm going to
15:33:12 write const just once. And then, and
15:33:15 this is the new bit, I'm going to

15:33:40 film. Let's hit save and see what
15:33:43 happens. And it looks like it is
15:33:45 working. Down in the console, we have
15:33:47 got exactly the same sentence. And the
15:33:50 code we've written is so much drier,
15:33:52 easier to write, and it's so much
15:33:54 quicker. Const and favorite film have
15:33:57 gone from being written five times to
15:33:59 being written just once each. And all of
15:34:02 these property variable names here like
15:34:04 title, year, these were written twice in

15:41:22 milliseconds. So fair enough if you said
15:41:25 5 seconds. But actually the answer is a
15:41:27 bit more complex than that. And as you
15:41:29 continue learning and using JavaScript,
15:41:32 it's useful to understand a bit more
15:41:34 about the language and why it behaves
15:41:35 the way it does. So I want to talk a bit
15:41:38 about how JavaScript works under the
15:41:39 hood. And that means investigating the
15:41:41 call stack, the event loop, and various
15:41:43 other goodies. You might have heard this
15:41:45 that JavaScript is a singlethreaded
15:41:48 non-blocking language. And honestly,
15:41:50 that might not mean too much to you
15:41:51 right now. It certainly didn't mean much
15:41:53 to me when I first heard it. So, let's
15:41:55 see if we can discover what it really
15:41:56 means. So, JavaScript has a runtime
15:41:59 environment. The most famous one is V8,
15:42:01 which is used by Chromebased browsers,
15:42:03 but there are others. The JavaScript
15:42:05 runtime environment gives us the heap
15:42:07 where memory is allocated. And luckily,
15:42:10 it does all of that for us. And so, as
15:42:12 JavaScript devs, we don't have to worry
15:42:14 about memory in the same way that you do
15:42:15 when working with some other languages.
15:42:17 So, we don't need to know more about the
15:42:19 heap for now. The JavaScript runtime
15:42:21 environment also gives us the call stack
15:42:23 and this is where code executes. So
15:42:25 let's deal with JavaScript being
15:42:27 singlethreaded because that means the
15:42:29 call stack can only execute one piece of
15:42:31 code at a time in its single thread.
15:42:34 Let's bring in an editor. I've got some
15:42:36 JavaScript here and it will be executed
15:42:38 from the top down. So we start off with
15:42:40 this line here where we're taking
15:42:42 control of an element. Then we'll
15:42:43 execute this line. Now we're getting a
15:42:46 random number and here we come to a set
15:42:48 timeout and it's got a 5,000 millisecond
15:42:51 delay. So as JavaScript is
15:42:52 singlethreaded, you might expect that we
15:42:54 would have to wait now for 5 seconds
15:42:57 before this final piece of code, this
15:42:59 console.log can execute. But we know
15:43:01 that's not the case. We've seen already
15:43:03 that this final piece of code will
15:43:05 execute before this code inside the set
15:43:08 timeout. And this is where we get to the
15:43:10 idea of JavaScript being singlethreaded
15:43:12 yet also non-blocking. But then if
15:43:14 JavaScript is singlethreaded, how does
15:43:16 it manage to juggle two tasks at once?
15:43:19 Well, it's got some friends provided by
15:43:21 the browser that give it a helping hand.
15:43:23 It's got web APIs. It's got a task Q and
15:43:27 it's got an event loop. And these are
15:43:29 not part of the JavaScript language, but
15:43:31 they work with it to give it
15:43:32 asynchronous powers, i.e. the power to
15:43:35 execute code while code that would
15:43:37 otherwise be holding things up is being
15:43:39 looked after in the background. So while
15:43:41 JavaScript is singlethreaded, we can
15:43:43 still multitask. And the kinds of things
15:43:45 it can do asynchronously include timers
15:43:47 like set time out and set interval,
15:43:50 fetching data across the network, which
15:43:52 can be super timeconuming, and working
15:43:54 with promises, which you might well not
15:43:56 have come across yet. Don't worry about
15:43:58 that at all. You will learn about them
15:44:00 later. Okay, let's take a look at the
15:44:02 actual process. So right here, I've got
15:44:04 some JavaScript ready to execute. We've
15:44:07 got a console down here. We're just
15:44:08 logging out first, second, and third.
15:44:11 But second is inside this set timeout.
15:44:13 And we've got our call stack, web APIs,
15:44:16 a task Q, and an event loop. So let's
15:44:18 execute our first bit of code. It's this
15:44:21 console.log, which is just going to log
15:44:22 out first. So we push it into the stack.
15:44:25 And you can think of the stack as being
15:44:26 a bit like an array-like object where
15:44:28 things can be pushed onto it and popped
15:44:30 off the top of it. In the stack, our
15:44:32 code gets executed. We log out first.
15:44:35 the stack can be cleared and our first
15:44:37 task is done. And just to be clear, this
15:44:39 is a simplification. If we had more
15:44:41 complex code with functions doing lots
15:44:43 of tasks or calling other functions, the
15:44:45 call stack would be busier. But let's
15:44:47 keep it simple. Okay, next we come to
15:44:49 the set timeout. So now we need to work
15:44:52 asynchronously. The code goes into the
15:44:54 stack and here things get interesting.
15:44:56 The stack passes set timeouts function
15:44:58 to a web API and there it gets a timer
15:45:01 which is going to count the 5,000
15:45:03 milliseconds. Now the stack can pop that
15:45:06 task off and we move on to the next line
15:45:08 of code which is the third console.log.
15:45:11 We pass it over to the stack. It
15:45:13 executes. So we log out third and then
15:45:15 the stack gets cleared and that line of
15:45:18 code is done. Back at the web API, the
15:45:20 timer counts the milliseconds and then
15:45:22 it's done. So it passes the function
15:45:24 down to the task Q. The function is the
15:45:27 only code in the task Q. So it's at the
15:45:29 front of the queue. And here comes the
15:45:31 event loop. Its job is to keep an eye on
15:45:33 the task Q and the stack. And when the
15:45:35 stack is empty, it will grab the task
15:45:37 from the top of the task Q and push it
15:45:39 to the stack.
15:45:42 And there it executes. So we log out
15:45:44 second, the stack empties, and we're
15:45:46 done. And we can see the order of
15:45:48 execution. First, third, and second.
15:45:51 Right, let's pause here a moment because
15:45:53 there are a few complexities I think we
15:45:55 should deal with. Firstly, what are web
15:45:58 APIs? Well, web APIs are provided by the
15:46:01 browser. They're not part of the
15:46:02 JavaScript language, and they have
15:46:05 functionality for things like DOM
15:46:07 manipulation, data requests, timers like
15:46:10 set timeout and set interval, as we've
15:46:12 seen, and plenty of other things
15:46:14 besides. So, in the case of our set
15:46:16 timeout, the relevant web API can hold
15:46:18 our code, count the milliseconds until
15:46:20 its time is up, and then pass it to the
15:46:22 task Q. And as the web APIs are not part
15:46:25 of JavaScript, while you interact with
15:46:27 them all the time, you never actually
15:46:29 work with them directly. So really, it's
15:46:32 just a good idea to know that they're
15:46:33 there and to know what they're doing.
15:46:35 You don't need to take a deeper dive
15:46:37 into it than that. Now, things don't
15:46:39 always go as smoothly as in our first
15:46:41 example. Imagine this is our codebase.
15:46:44 I've got a console.log, a second
15:46:46 console.log inside the set timeout, but
15:46:48 down here I'm calling a superheavy
15:46:50 function which is mining Bitcoin. So we

15:46:54 first item to the call stack. It gets
15:46:56 executed and logged out. Then we come on
15:46:59 to the set timeout. It goes to the call
15:47:01 stack. We know the function gets passed
15:47:03 to the web API. The timer starts. We
15:47:05 clear the stack. And that brings us on
15:47:07 to the function where we're mining
15:47:09 Bitcoin. Let's pass that into the call
15:47:11 stack. Okay, that function is going to
15:47:13 take a long time. So back at the web
15:47:15 APIs, the timer finishes and the
15:47:18 function is passed down to the task Q.
15:47:20 But the event loop which is keeping an
15:47:22 eye on the call stack and the task Q

15:48:01 this timer. Let's see what's about to

15:48:06 So the web API passes the function to
15:48:08 the task Q. But look, it's not alone in
15:48:11 the task Q. It's got two tasks ahead of
15:48:13 it. Tasks A and B, which could be a
15:48:16 couple of functions, for example. So now
15:48:18 this function has to wait for mine
15:48:20 Bitcoin to complete so that the stack
15:48:23 can be emptied and then it's also going
15:48:25 to have to wait for task A and task B to
15:48:28 complete through the call stack before
15:48:30 it will get called. So it's being
15:48:32 significantly delayed. And that by the
15:48:34 way is a good thing. It's what we want
15:48:37 to happen. If this code here was just

16:12:14 good to know it's there. Okay, let's

16:12:41 before we do that, let's rewind. I'm
16:12:43 asking you about this code because I
16:12:45 want to talk about hoisting. And
16:12:47 hoisting is the behavior where variable
16:12:49 and function declarations are moved to
16:12:52 the top of their containing scope during
16:12:54 the compilation phase before code
16:12:57 execution. What that means in practice
16:12:59 is that this function right here behaves
16:13:02 as if it's been declared right up here
16:13:04 or sort of. Let's check it out with real
16:13:07 code. And actually, I'm going to comment
16:13:09 out some of these so we can take them
16:13:11 one by one.
16:13:13 So remember as we declare these
16:13:14 functions and the variable we'll come to
16:13:16 later they're hoisted to the top of the
16:13:18 scope which in this case is the global
16:13:21 scope. So for this first function it
16:13:23 should be fairly obvious JavaScript is
16:13:25 read from the top down. We're declaring
16:13:27 the function right here and invoking the
16:13:29 function here. When we hit save of
16:13:32 course it works. So no great puzzle
16:13:34 about that. Let's move on to the second
16:13:37 example. Now we're invoking the function
16:13:40 here, but we're not declaring the
16:13:42 function until afterwards down here. Is
16:13:44 this going to work? Yes, it is. So this
16:13:48 is an example of hoisting in action. The
16:13:50 function declaration has been moved to
16:13:52 the top of its scope. So it's
16:13:54 effectively above the function
16:13:56 invocation. That all happens under the
16:13:58 hood at compilation. So when we invoke
16:14:01 the function right here, JavaScript
16:14:03 already knows about it. Okay, let's take
16:14:05 the third example. When we hit save,
16:14:09 things go wrong. We get the error cannot
16:14:11 access traffic info before
16:14:13 initialization. And by the way, that
16:14:15 would be the same if this were a const.
16:14:19 When it comes to scope and hoisting, let
16:14:21 and const behave the same. And so we
16:14:23 can't access these variables before we
16:14:26 initialize them. And some people would
16:14:28 just say that the let and the const
16:14:30 JavaScript are not hoisted. But that is
16:14:32 not quite true. If we try to log out a
16:14:35 variable that simply hasn't been
16:14:36 declared,
16:14:39 we get a different error. Random var is
16:14:42 not defined. So it's not that JavaScript
16:14:44 doesn't know this code exists. As it
16:14:47 says, we cannot access traffic info
16:14:49 before initialization. And why is that?
16:14:52 Well, that is because traffic info
16:14:54 exists in what's called the temporal
16:14:56 dead zone. And the temporal dead zone is
16:14:59 a semi-zombie like state like those
16:15:02 first few minutes of an early morning
16:15:03 where you know who you are but you can't
16:15:05 quite function. In code, it's literally
16:15:07 the space where the variable has been
16:15:09 declared and JavaScript knows about it
16:15:12 because the declaration has been hoisted
16:15:14 but it's not available to access until
16:15:17 it's been initialized. Just to explain
16:15:19 that we did say variable and function
16:15:22 declarations are moved to the top of
16:15:24 their containing scope, not invocations
16:15:26 or initializations. So what we're
16:15:29 actually hoisting is essentially just
16:15:31 this part right here. And then we're
16:15:33 invoking it down here. That's what's
16:15:36 effectively happening under the hood.
16:15:38 And that's why JavaScript knows that
16:15:40 traffic info exists, but we can't
16:15:42 actually access the part that we're
16:15:44 adding in here, the initialization,
16:15:46 because that is not being hoisted. Okay,
16:15:48 let's just put that back to how it was.
16:15:51 And just out of interest, let's see what
16:15:52 happens if we try to use var.
16:15:55 Well, var is a little bit more
16:15:57 forgiving. We get undefined rather than
16:15:58 an error, but that doesn't matter
16:16:00 because you're not going to use var
16:16:01 anyway. But that's the behavior you'll
16:16:03 see if you're working on legacy code. So
16:16:06 the important takehome from this is that
16:16:08 while function declarations, consts and
16:16:10 lets are hoisted, only function
16:16:12 declarations can be used before they've
16:16:15 been declared. Lets and const will only
16:16:18 be usable after initialization. Now as
16:16:21 we go on in this course, we're going to
16:16:22 see other types of functions, function
16:16:24 expressions, arrow functions, and we'll
16:16:26 also see classes and generators, and
16:16:28 where appropriate, we'll talk about
16:16:30 hoisting in those cases. But from now
16:16:32 on, when you learn a new concept, you
16:16:34 know how you can quickly and easily
16:16:36 check if it's hoisted or not.

16:30:02 expression. And this expression will
16:30:04 execute if the condition is fullc. So

16:55:36 got another similar method that we want
16:55:37 to look at which will execute code
16:55:40 repeatedly at a given time frequency. So
16:55:43 let's come on to that next.
16:55:47 How long will it be before this code
16:55:49 executes? We've set a delay of 5,000
16:55:51 milliseconds. So, fair enough if you
16:55:54 said 5 seconds. But actually, the answer
16:55:56 is a bit more complex than that. And as
16:55:58 you continue learning and using
16:56:00 JavaScript, it's useful to understand a
16:56:03 bit more about the language and why it
16:56:04 behaves the way it does. So, I want to
16:56:07 talk a bit about how JavaScript works
16:56:08 under the hood. And that means
16:56:10 investigating the call stack, the event
16:56:12 loop, and various other goodies. You
16:56:14 might have heard this that JavaScript is
16:56:16 a singlethreaded non-blocking language.
16:56:18 And honestly, that might not mean too
16:56:20 much to you right now. It certainly
16:56:22 didn't mean much to me when I first
16:56:23 heard it. So, let's see if we can
16:56:25 discover what it really means. So,
16:56:27 JavaScript has a runtime environment.
16:56:29 The most famous one is V8, which is used
16:56:31 by Chromebased browsers, but there are
16:56:33 others. The JavaScript runtime
16:56:35 environment gives us the heap where
16:56:37 memory is allocated. And luckily, it
16:56:39 does all of that for us. And so as
16:56:41 JavaScript devs, we don't have to worry
16:56:43 about memory in the same way that you do
16:56:45 when working with some other languages.
16:56:46 So we don't need to know more about the
16:56:48 heap for now. The JavaScript runtime
16:56:50 environment also gives us the call
16:56:52 stack. And this is where code executes.
16:56:54 So let's deal with JavaScript being
16:56:56 singlethreaded because that means the
16:56:59 call stack can only execute one piece of
16:57:01 code at a time in its single thread.
16:57:04 Let's bring in an editor. I've got some
16:57:06 JavaScript here and it will be executed
16:57:07 from the top down. So we start off with
16:57:10 this line here where we're taking
16:57:11 control of an element. Then we'll
16:57:13 execute this line. Now we're getting a
16:57:15 random number. And here we come to a set
16:57:18 timeout. And it's got a 5,000
16:57:20 millisecond delay. So as JavaScript is
16:57:22 singlethreaded, you might expect that we
16:57:24 would have to wait now for 5 seconds
16:57:26 before this final piece of code, this
16:57:28 console.log, can execute. But we know
16:57:31 that's not the case. We've seen already
16:57:33 that this final piece of code will
16:57:34 execute before this code inside the set
16:57:37 timeout. And this is where we get to the
16:57:39 idea of JavaScript being singlethreaded
16:57:42 yet also non-blocking. But then if
16:57:44 JavaScript is singlethreaded, how does
16:57:46 it manage to juggle two tasks at once?
16:57:48 Well, it's got some friends provided by
16:57:50 the browser that give it a helping hand.
16:57:53 It's got web APIs. It's got a task Q and
16:57:56 it's got an event loop. And these are
16:57:58 not part of the JavaScript language, but
16:58:00 they work with it to give it
16:58:02 asynchronous powers, i.e. the power to
16:58:04 execute code while code that would
16:58:06 otherwise be holding things up is being
16:58:08 looked after in the background. So while
16:58:10 JavaScript is single threaded, we can
16:58:12 still multitask and the kinds of things
16:58:14 that can do asynchronously include
16:58:16 timers like set time out and set
16:58:18 interval, fetching data across the
16:58:21 network which can be super timeconuming
16:58:23 and working with promises which you
16:58:25 might well not have come across yet.
16:58:27 Don't worry about that at all. You will
16:58:29 learn about them later. Okay, let's take
16:58:31 a look at the actual process. So right
16:58:33 here I've got some JavaScript ready to
16:58:35 execute. We've got a console down here.
16:58:37 We're just logging out first, second,
16:58:39 and third. But second is inside this set
16:58:42 timeout. And we've got our call stack,
16:58:44 web APIs, a task Q, and an event loop.
16:58:47 So let's execute our first bit of code.
16:58:50 It's this console.log, which is just
16:58:51 going to log out first. So we push it
16:58:54 into the stack. And you can think of the
16:58:55 stack as being a bit like an array-like
16:58:57 object where things can be pushed onto
16:58:59 it and popped off the top of it. in the
16:59:01 stack. Our code gets executed. We log
16:59:04 out first. The stack can be cleared and
16:59:06 our first task is done. And just to be
16:59:08 clear, this is a simplification. If we
16:59:10 had more complex code with functions
16:59:12 doing lots of tasks or calling other
16:59:14 functions, the call stack would be
16:59:15 busier. But let's keep it simple. Okay.
16:59:18 Next, we come to the set timeout. So now
16:59:20 we need to work asynchronously. The code
16:59:22 goes into the stack. And here things get
16:59:24 interesting. The stack passes set
16:59:27 timeouts function to a web API. And
16:59:29 there it gets a timer which is going to
16:59:31 count the 5,000 milliseconds. Now the
16:59:34 stack can pop that task off. And we move
16:59:36 on to the next line of code which is the
16:59:39 third console.log. We pass it over to
16:59:41 the stack. It executes. So we log out
16:59:44 third and then the stack gets cleared
16:59:46 and that line of code is done. Back at
16:59:48 the web API, the timer counts the
16:59:50 milliseconds and then it's done. So it
16:59:53 passes the function down to the task Q.
16:59:55 The function is the only code in the
16:59:57 task Q. So it's at the front of the
16:59:59 queue. And here comes the event loop.
17:00:01 Its job is to keep an eye on the task Q
17:00:03 and the stack. And when the stack is
17:00:05 empty, it will grab the task from the
17:00:07 top of the task Q and push it to the
17:00:09 stack.
17:00:11 And there it executes. So we log out
17:00:13 second, the stack empties. And we're
17:00:16 done. And we can see the order of
17:00:18 execution. First, third, and second.
17:00:21 Right. Let's pause here a moment because
17:00:23 there are a few complexities I think we
17:00:25 should deal with. Firstly, what are web
17:00:27 APIs? Well, web APIs are provided by the
17:00:30 browser. They're not part of the
17:00:32 JavaScript language, and they have
17:00:34 functionality for things like DOM
17:00:36 manipulation, data requests, timers like
17:00:39 set timeout and set interval as we've
17:00:41 seen, and plenty of other things
17:00:43 besides. So, in the case of our set
17:00:45 timeout, the relevant web API can hold
17:00:48 our code, count the milliseconds until
17:00:50 its time is up, and then pass it to the
17:00:52 task Q. And as the web APIs are not part
17:00:54 of JavaScript, while you interact with
17:00:56 them all the time, you never actually
17:00:58 work with them directly. So really, it's
17:01:01 just a good idea to know that they're
17:01:02 there and to know what they're doing.
17:01:04 You don't need to take a deeper dive
17:01:06 into it than that. Now, things don't
17:01:08 always go as smoothly as in our first
17:01:10 example. Imagine this is our codebase.
17:01:13 I've got a console.log, a second
17:01:15 console.log inside the set timeout, but
17:01:18 down here I'm calling a superheavy
17:01:20 function which is mining Bitcoin. So we

17:01:26 executed and logged out. Then we come on

17:01:37 to the function where we're mining
17:01:38 Bitcoin. Let's pass that into the call
17:01:41 stack. Okay, that function is going to
17:01:43 take a long time. So back at the web
17:01:45 APIs, the timer finishes and the

17:01:50 But the event loop which is keeping an

17:02:35 So the web API passes the function to
17:02:38 the task Q. But look, it's not alone in
17:02:41 the task Q. It's got two tasks ahead of
17:02:43 it. Tasks A and B, which could be a
17:02:46 couple of functions, for example. So now
17:02:48 this function has to wait for mine
17:02:49 Bitcoin to complete so that the stack
17:02:52 can be emptied and then it's also going
17:02:54 to have to wait for task A and task B to
17:02:57 complete through the call stack before
17:02:59 it will get called. So it's being
17:03:02 significantly delayed. And that by the
17:03:04 way is a good thing. It's what we want
17:03:06 to happen. If this code here was just
17:03:08 shoved into the call stack as soon as
17:03:10 the 5,000 milliseconds had elapsed, it
17:03:12 would get mixed up with any other code
17:03:13 that was already in there and that would
17:03:16 cause chaos. And so although we only

17:13:33 so this is a great time to take a break.
17:13:39 In JavaScript, a constructor gives you
17:13:41 an object. That's fundamentally what
17:13:44 constructors do. But there are many
17:13:46 varying use cases for constructors.
17:13:49 Firstly, let's break them down into two
17:13:51 categories. We have got inbuilt
17:13:53 constructors that come as part of
17:13:55 JavaScript. So, these provide objects in
17:13:58 various predetermined formats like date
17:14:00 objects, error objects, and we've got
17:14:03 objects for each of the data types. and
17:14:05 we're going to have a look at date
17:14:06 objects and error objects in this scrim
17:14:08 and the next scrim. And we'll also have
17:14:10 a quick look at using constructors to

18:13:26 functions that I want to say. So when
18:13:28 you're ready let's move on.
18:13:33 You might well have heard that in
18:13:35 JavaScript functions are considered
18:13:36 first class citizens. What this means is
18:13:39 that functions in JavaScript have the
18:13:41 same capabilities as other data types
18:13:43 like numbers, strings or objects.
18:13:45 Specifically, that means functions can
18:13:47 be assigned to variables. And we've seen
18:13:50 that already with function expressions.
18:13:52 Functions can also be passed as
18:13:54 arguments to other functions. And we've
18:13:56 seen that several times already as well.
18:13:58 And that brings me to a quiz. Can you
18:14:01 list some of the times we've passed
18:14:02 functions as arguments to other
18:14:04 functions? And you can just list them
18:14:06 here. 1 2 3. And feel free to go on if
18:14:09 you can think of more examples.
18:14:13 Okay. So we've definitely done that with
18:14:14 the array methods. So map for each
18:14:19 reduce etc. We've also seen that with
18:14:22 the asynchronous methods set timeout
18:14:26 and set interval
18:14:28 and lastly we've seen it with the event
18:14:30 listeners. So in all of those cases we
18:14:33 have passed functions as arguments to
18:14:35 other functions. So if we take say the
18:14:38 for each we have an array perhaps an
18:14:41 array of names we call the for each
18:14:44 method and what we pass to for each is a
18:14:47 function and we tell for each to take
18:14:50 every element in the array and do
18:14:52 something with it. Now the functions
18:14:55 that we pass into other functions like
18:14:57 this for each method are called callback
18:15:00 functions. And that's a somewhat odd
18:15:02 thing to call them and it's not that
18:15:03 intuitive, but they're called that
18:15:05 because they are called back on each
18:15:06 element in the array we are working on
18:15:08 in the case of an array method like for
18:15:10 each or in the case of a set timeout or
18:15:12 set interval. They're called when the
18:15:14 delay is up or perhaps when an event has
18:15:16 been detected. So the name callback
18:15:19 function is somewhat unintuitive but
18:15:21 just remember it's a function which is
18:15:22 passed to another function to be
18:15:24 utilized when that function deems
18:15:27 necessary. Now we can also make our own
18:15:29 functions that expect a callback
18:15:31 function as a parameter and we're going
18:15:32 to make a really basic example of that
18:15:34 right now. So this function will notify
18:15:37 a user. It's going to take in a call
18:15:40 back function as a parameter. Now I
18:15:42 could of course call that anything. I
18:15:44 could call it call back. That seems very

18:30:15 because an API can do multiple things.

18:31:46 their system or bringing it back to

18:34:48 the program, the JavaScript that someone

18:48:27 need to use that API's URL. The URL is

18:48:32 desired resource is located. The URL for

18:49:07 kind of resource you're getting from

18:49:12 this fictitious Scriber API will start

18:59:23 what we're going to talk about next.
18:59:27 Let's get to the bottom of promises.
18:59:30 We've been using them without really
18:59:31 mentioning them in the past few scrims.
18:59:33 And I want to investigate them using the
18:59:36 analogy of a job interview. And I
18:59:38 borrowed this analogy straight from
18:59:40 another course by Bob Zerroll. When you
18:59:42 go to a job interview, you typically
18:59:44 don't anticipate it concluding with
18:59:46 either you've got the job or we're
18:59:48 choosing another candidate. More often
18:59:50 than not, the interview wraps up with
18:59:52 something like we'll contact you within
18:59:54 a week to let you know. And essentially,
18:59:57 this is a promise with a time frame. And
18:59:59 this concept is pretty similar to how
19:00:01 promises work in JavaScript. If that
19:00:03 were JavaScript, that promise we'll let
19:00:06 you know within a week would be in one
19:00:08 of three states. The first state is
19:00:10 known as the pending state, which simply
19:00:13 means that the promise hasn't been
19:00:14 completed yet. It's still in process.
19:00:17 And we can relate this to the promises
19:00:18 we've encountered when using the fetch
19:00:20 method. When we call the fetch with a
19:00:22 URL, it takes some time for the request
19:00:24 to be sent, for the server to handle it,
19:00:26 and then for the response to be sent
19:00:28 back over the internet. During this
19:00:30 time, the promise remains in a pending
19:00:32 state. From this pending state, a
19:00:34 promise can transition to one of two
19:00:36 other states. The one we're most
19:00:38 familiar with so far is the fulfilled or
19:00:41 resolved state. This is when the promise
19:00:43 has been successfully completed. And
19:00:45 just to be clear, you'll sometimes hear
19:00:47 it called resolved and sometimes hear it
19:00:49 called fulfilled. It's the same thing.
19:00:51 Returning to our earlier analogy of the
19:00:54 job interview, we'll let you know within
19:00:56 a week. I want you just to pause for a
19:00:58 moment and consider what the fulfilled
19:01:00 state of that promise would look like in
19:01:02 a job interview scenario. Just think
19:01:04 about that for a moment. And I'm going
19:01:06 to note down the question for you.
19:01:10 If you said the resolved state would be
19:01:12 that we got the job, you'd be wrong. The
19:01:14 promise was we'll let you know within a
19:01:17 week, not that we'll let you have a
19:01:18 positive answer in a week. So they might
19:01:21 get in touch within a week to say you've
19:01:22 got the job or it might be to say thanks
19:01:24 but no thanks. Both of those are
19:01:27 fulfilled or resolved promises. And
19:01:29 that's important to understand. So a
19:01:31 resolved or fulfilled promise, the
19:01:34 promise was completed as promised. But
19:01:36 bear in mind that doesn't necessarily
19:01:38 mean you got what you wanted. Okay. So
19:01:40 what about the third state which is
19:01:42 rejected? I want you to think about that
19:01:44 with the same analogy. Just give that
19:01:47 some thought.
19:01:50 Well, again, if you said you didn't get
19:01:53 the job, that would be wrong. This would
19:01:55 simply mean that they did not get back
19:01:57 to you within a week. So the promise was
19:02:00 not completed as promised. Now if we
19:02:02 have a look at the code that we wrote
19:02:04 before, we've actually got two promises
19:02:06 here. As we've seen, if we comment out
19:02:09 this line and we log out the response,
19:02:12 but we don't await it. We just see what
19:02:14 fetch brings us in the console. We see
19:02:17 we've got a promise. Now let's await
19:02:20 this fetch. We will convert the response
19:02:23 to JSON, but we won't await it. Let's
19:02:25 just log out the data. And again, we get
19:02:28 another promise. And in this case, both
19:02:30 of the promises are resolving just fine.
19:02:32 We're successfully resolving the first
19:02:34 promise and storing our response in this
19:02:37 const. And then when we call the JSON
19:02:39 method on the response, that promise is
19:02:41 resolving. We're storing it in this
19:02:43 const data and logging that data to the
19:02:46 console. So, up until now, we've only
19:02:48 really seen promises in the pending and
19:02:51 the resolved or fulfilled state. We

19:03:00 error or some other similar problem. And

19:35:48 resolved. So, this 50% chance of

19:35:53 three times in a row. So, what we're

19:35:58 We're passing in an array of our
19:36:00 promises and we can pass in as many as

20:22:18 I've mentioned tokens a few times

20:22:22 Well, if we have a look at the last
20:22:24 response we got back, down at the bottom
20:22:26 we have usage and it tells us that there
20:22:29 were 44 prompt tokens, 56 completion
20:22:32 tokens, and that the total tokens were
20:22:35 100. Now, this final figure is probably
20:22:38 the most important because you pay for
20:22:40 every token. So, what we know so far is
20:22:42 that our prompt, which is basically what
20:22:45 we've got right here, cost us 44 tokens,
20:22:48 and the completion that we see right
20:22:50 here was 56 tokens. So, what actually
20:22:53 are tokens? Well, a token is not a
20:22:56 character, a word, or a syllable. It's
20:22:58 not as simple as that. It's a chunk of
20:23:00 text of no specific length, but on
20:23:02 average, according to OpenAI, it's
20:23:04 around four characters.
20:23:07 Now, you can use this tokenizer tool
20:23:09 from OpenAI. And this screenshot is of
20:23:11 course a link. And what this will do is
20:23:13 show you how a piece of text breaks down
20:23:15 into tokens. So you just put some text
20:23:17 in here. And I'll paste in our most
20:23:19 recent completion. And instantly we get
20:23:22 a token output. So each color block is a
20:23:26 token. And you'll notice that the word
20:23:28 quantum is in fact two tokens. One for
20:23:31 the quant, one for the um. You'll also
20:23:34 notice that normally the space preceding
20:23:36 a word is included in that token. and
20:23:39 that punctuation like full stops or
20:23:42 periods are their own token. And this is
20:23:45 coming out as 56 tokens. And if we have
20:23:47 a look down here, well, that concurs.
20:23:50 The model told us that it had generated
20:23:52 56 tokens. Now, why does this matter to
20:23:54 you? Well, tokens cost credit. The more
20:23:57 tokens you use, the more money you
20:23:59 spend. Tokens need processing. So, the
20:24:02 more tokens you use, the more lag time
20:24:04 you get, the slower your app will be.
20:24:06 So, the main takehome from this is that
20:24:08 keeping token numbers low saves your
20:24:10 users time and saves you money. Now, how
20:24:13 much does a token cost? Well, I can't
20:24:15 really answer that because it depends on
20:24:17 which model you're using and also prices
20:24:20 are changing quite rapidly. So, I would
20:24:23 recommend that you do some research in
20:24:24 the OpenAI docs to find the specific
20:24:27 token cost for the model you're using at
20:24:29 that time. Now, you can control the
20:24:31 number of tokens that you use with the
20:24:33 max token setting, but it is a rather
20:24:36 blunt tool. What it does is it limits
20:24:39 the number of tokens the model will
20:24:40 output. It does not affect the size of
20:24:43 your input. So, what you're controlling
20:24:46 is this number right here, but not this

20:26:29 Okay. Some advice for setting max
20:26:32 tokens, then. So, max tokens does not
20:26:34 allow us to control how concise a text
20:26:37 is. It's not actually changing the
20:26:39 quality of the output. All it's doing is

20:26:44 So, it is a very, very blunt tool. If

20:26:49 enough tokens for a full response. So,

20:48:24 Next,
20:48:28 let's take a look at fine-tuning with
20:48:30 the OpenAI API. So, what actually is
20:48:33 fine-tuning? Well, fine-tuning involves
20:48:35 giving a standard pre-trained model such
20:48:38 as GPT4 a specific data set to enhance
20:48:41 its performance on a particular task. In
20:48:43 the fine-tuning process, the weights of
20:48:45 the model will actually be adjusted so
20:48:47 you get more of the results you want.
20:48:49 But if you don't know what weights in AI
20:48:51 models are, don't worry at all. That's
20:48:53 all happening under the hood, and you
20:48:54 don't need to know about it for
20:48:56 fine-tuning or using the API in general.
20:48:58 So, what are the use cases for
20:49:00 fine-tuning? Well, you might want to
20:49:02 achieve a specific tone and style. So,
20:49:04 you can use fine-tuning to teach the
20:49:06 model how you want it to respond. You
20:49:08 might want your output in a specific
20:49:10 format like some JSON data for example
20:49:12 which needs to fit a particular pattern.
20:49:14 You could use it with function calling.
20:49:16 Fine-tuning can improve function
20:49:18 calling. And if you haven't looked at
20:49:19 function calling in AI yet, don't worry
20:49:21 at all. We won't be talking about it
20:49:23 more in this overview of fine-tuning.
20:49:25 Now, as well as those three use cases,
20:49:27 there are also some financial
20:49:28 motivations. You can save money on the
20:49:31 fuchsia approach because if you want to
20:49:33 give the model loads of examples with
20:49:34 each call to the API, the prompt sizes
20:49:37 become huge and you use loads of tokens.
20:49:39 Fine-tuning effectively allows you to do
20:49:42 that once and then use the fine-tune
20:49:44 model with much shorter prompts and
20:49:46 therefore lower token usage in the long
20:49:48 term. You can also save money by
20:49:50 downgrading the model. So you might get
20:49:52 the same performance for less money if
20:49:54 you use a fine-tuned older model
20:49:56 compared to a non-fineetuned bleeding
20:49:58 edge model. So that is also something to
20:50:00 consider. Here's an important caveat.
20:50:03 According to OpenAI, fine-tuning should
20:50:05 be used as a last resort. First, you
20:50:07 should work on your prompt design. Then
20:50:09 you should adjust any settings like
20:50:10 temperature to see if you can improve
20:50:12 results that way. Of course, use the
20:50:14 fshot approach to give some examples and
20:50:16 then fine-tune only if the above doesn't
