36:51:00 natural next step in our discussion
36:51:03 about side effects and specifically a
36:51:06 hook that React gives us called use
36:51:08 effect. So, let's dive right into
36:51:10 learning use effect and see how it can
36:51:12 help us solve this problem that we're
36:51:14 facing.
36:51:17 Let's kick off the discussion about use
36:51:19 effect by looking once again at what
36:51:22 React's primary tasks are. As we've
36:51:24 discussed, the main thing that it's in
36:51:26 charge of is working with the DOM and
36:51:28 the browser in order to render user
36:51:30 interface to the page. Very helpfully.
36:51:32 Along with that, it's going to manage
36:51:34 any state for us between render cycles,
36:51:36 which gives us developers a way for a
36:51:39 value to be remembered from one render
36:51:41 to the next. And because we have state
36:51:44 and we have props, React is in charge of
36:51:46 updating the UI. Once again, kind of
36:51:48 like we talked about in the first point,
36:51:50 it will update that UI whenever the
36:51:52 state or the incoming props change.
36:51:54 There's a lot of other things that React
36:51:56 of course is doing behind the scenes,
36:51:57 but I think it would be fair to boil
36:51:59 down all of the very detailed things
36:52:01 that it does to these kind of main
36:52:03 tasks. Well, this naturally leads us to
36:52:05 ask what can't React handle on its own?
36:52:08 And primarily React can't really do much
36:52:11 regarding outside effects. Some examples
36:52:13 of this are interfacing with local
36:52:15 storage. If we're relying on a value
36:52:17 from local storage and that value
36:52:19 changes one way or another, React isn't
36:52:22 automatically going to update itself
36:52:24 based on the changes from local storage.
36:52:26 I guess let me also be clear about local
36:52:28 storage and the rest of the items in

36:52:32 these tools with a React application.
36:52:34 I'm simply saying React isn't

36:52:38 we need to connect these things manually
36:52:40 ourselves. Another example of an outside
36:52:42 effect that React doesn't automatically
36:52:44 connect to is an API or any kind of
36:52:46 database interaction. We need to
36:52:48 manually call out to an API and receive
36:52:51 the data that comes back in order to

37:08:08 type down your answers. For question
37:08:10 number one, it's been a little bit of
37:08:11 time since we talked about functional
37:08:13 programming and pure functions. So don't
37:08:15 hesitate to open up that lesson on
37:08:17 functional programming in another tab or
37:08:19 something and review that before
37:08:20 answering this question on the quiz. So
37:08:22 go ahead and answer the questions in
37:08:24 this quiz.
37:08:29 Okay, number one, in what way are React
37:08:31 components meant to be pure functions?
37:08:34 And actually, I'm going to pull up my
37:08:36 old slides on this one right here. The
37:08:39 idea of the pure functions was to say
37:08:41 that a component when it's given certain
37:08:43 props should always return the same user
37:08:45 interface. And the same thing holds true
37:08:48 of a state change or any internal state
37:08:50 in the component given the same state
37:08:52 values or the same props values it
37:08:54 should always return the same user
37:08:56 interface. And so that's the main idea
37:08:59 of pure functions in React. So given the
37:09:02 same props or state, a component will
37:09:04 always return the same content or the
37:09:06 same UI that gets rendered on the page.
37:09:08 Another aspect of having pure functions
37:09:10 in React is that rendering your
37:09:12 component or running that component
37:09:14 function will never affect any kind of
37:09:16 outside system. We talked previously and
37:09:20 imagined a scenario where maybe our
37:09:22 component every single time it rendered
37:09:24 were to add an item to a list in a
37:09:28 database. This would be very blatantly
37:09:31 not a pure function because every time
37:09:33 it runs it adds a new item to a database
37:09:35 which means we are very much affecting
37:09:37 an outside system. So that's just
37:09:40 another important aspect of having pure
37:09:42 functions in React. That leads us very
37:09:44 naturally to what is a side effect in
37:09:46 React and what are some examples? Well,
37:09:48 really a side effect is any code that
37:09:51 affects or we could even say interacts
37:09:54 with an outside system. It's a little
37:09:57 nuanced, but even doing a get request to
37:10:00 an API is considered a side effect. Even
37:10:03 though we're not really making a change
37:10:04 to that outside system, simply
37:10:06 interacting with the outside system
37:10:08 makes it a side effect in the realm of
37:10:10 React components. Some examples of this
37:10:13 might include interacting with local
37:10:15 storage, anything dealing with an API or
37:10:19 making a connection like through
37:10:21 websockets. One we hadn't really talked
37:10:23 too much about was doing any kind of
37:10:25 manual DOM manipulation. All of these
37:10:28 are services or systems that exist
37:10:30 outside of React. And so interacting
37:10:33 with them inside of your components is
37:10:35 considered a side effect in React. So
37:10:38 number three, by contrast, what is not
37:10:40 considered a side effect in React? And
37:10:42 what are some examples? Well, in this
37:10:43 case, it's really anything that React is
37:10:46 in charge of. Some examples of which are
37:10:50 maintaining state, keeping the UI in
37:10:54 sync with whatever data you're working
37:10:55 with, rendering DOM elements,
37:10:59 and certainly there's a lot of other
37:11:01 things that React is in charge of, but
37:11:03 all of those would be considered not
37:11:05 side effects in React. All right, number
37:11:07 four. This is kind of the meat of this
37:11:09 quiz. When does React run your use
37:11:11 effect? Well, it will always run your
37:11:13 use effect code as soon as the component
37:11:16 loads or let's say renders. And more
37:11:19 specifically, it's when it renders for
37:11:21 the first time. This most commonly
37:11:23 happens when you first refresh your page
37:11:25 or launch your app and it gets loaded
37:11:27 into the browser for the first time.
37:11:29 Although, it could also be if you were
37:11:31 conditionally rendering something and it
37:11:33 was not present on the page before, but
37:11:35 then some state value changes and it
37:11:37 becomes present on the page. That would
37:11:38 be considered the first render for that
37:11:40 component. React will also run your use
37:11:43 effect function every time a component
37:11:45 rerenders assuming you have no
37:11:47 dependencies array. So on every rerender
37:11:50 of the component assuming no

37:11:53 this next one, when will it not rerun
37:11:56 the effect function? Well, that's going
37:11:57 to happen if between renders the values
37:12:00 in the dependencies array have not
37:12:02 changed. And lastly, how would you

37:12:07 Well, spoken more literally, it's the

37:12:13 way for React to know whether or not it
37:12:15 should rerun the effect function.

37:39:40 one final recap before we move on. Use
37:39:42 effect allows us to interact with
37:39:44 outside systems and potentially have
37:39:46 side effects inside of our React code,
37:39:48 which normally should be side
37:39:50 effectless. It takes two parameters. The
37:39:53 first one is the effect function and the
37:39:55 second one is the array of dependencies
37:39:57 that determines when that effect
37:39:59 function should run again. At bare
37:40:01 minimum, if we include an empty array,
37:40:03 it will only run the first time this
37:40:05 component mounts to the page because it
37:40:07 depends on nothing else to rerun. And at
37:40:10 the most, if we were to not include this
37:40:12 array at all, it would run on every
37:40:14 single render of this component. In
37:40:16 fact, we can delete this dependencies
37:40:18 array and hit save. will see that it's
37:40:21 going to run both resized and cleaning
37:40:24 up every single time this component
37:40:26 rerenders. You will pretty much always
37:40:28 want to include a dependencies array and
37:40:30 think very carefully about which values
37:40:32 you want to be watching in order to
37:40:33 trigger this effect again. Then lastly,
37:40:36 the callback function as the first
37:40:38 parameter to use effect is allowed to
37:40:39 return a function which will clean up
37:40:41 any of the side effects that you might
37:40:43 have created inside of your use effect
37:40:45 code. Returning this cleanup function is
37:40:47 something you will want to always be
37:40:49 aware of, but it isn't something you
37:40:51 always have to do. It's relatively
37:40:52 common to have your side effects not
37:40:55 have anything that's lingering and needs
37:40:57 to be cleaned up. So, just know that
37:40:58 it's here as a tool for you to use if
37:41:01 you need it, but it's not something that
37:41:02 you absolutely have to have to correctly
37:41:04 write a use effect. As always, if you
37:41:07 need to go through the content that

37:46:23 I wanted to go over a pretty truncated
37:46:25 view into refs in React and the use ref
37:46:29 hook. I say it's really truncated
37:46:30 because we're going to be going through
37:46:32 it pretty quickly only because I have an
37:46:34 improvement that I want to add to our
37:46:36 ChefClaude app that will require us to
37:46:39 use refs. And really quick before we
37:46:41 jump right into the slides, I did make a
37:46:43 couple really simple changes to our
37:46:45 ChefClaude app. Primarily, I moved some
37:46:47 of the components into a components
37:46:49 folder. I think before I maybe had the
37:46:52 main component and the header component
37:46:53 sitting outside in the project route,
37:46:55 but they felt a little more natural
37:46:57 inside the components folder. So, I
37:46:58 moved them there and then fixed the
37:47:00 references that we had with our relative
37:47:02 paths in each of those components that
37:47:04 needed them. Just wanted to make sure I
37:47:05 highlighted that before anyone got
37:47:07 confused why it was looking a little
37:47:08 different. Okay, so what are refs in
37:47:10 React and what is use ref? Refs are
37:47:12 fairly straightforward. They are very
37:47:14 similar to state except for two major
37:47:17 things. The first one is that you can
37:47:20 mutate refs directly with props or with
37:47:23 state. We know that it needs to be
37:47:24 immutable. And the term immutable might
37:47:27 not on its face make a lot of sense
37:47:29 because you might be thinking, well, we
37:47:30 do change state all the time. That's how
37:47:32 we trigger rerenders in React. But to
37:47:34 split hairs, when I'm talking about
37:47:36 mutable, I'm saying we're not saying
37:47:37 something like, okay, here's my
37:47:39 ingredients state, and I'm going to push
37:47:41 a new item to my list of ingredients.
37:47:44 This would be directly mutating state
37:47:46 when we know that we're supposed to use
37:47:48 the state setter function. Behind the
37:47:50 scenes, React is not just setting our
37:47:52 old ingredients array equal to something
37:47:55 new or doing something like push. It's
37:47:57 following a more appropriate functional
37:47:59 programming paradigm where behind the
37:48:02 scenes it's replacing the old version of
37:48:04 state with the new version of state and
37:48:06 then kind of queuing it up to rerender
37:48:08 the page. So, let me get rid of this
37:48:10 line. And refs on the other hand are
37:48:13 things that we can mutate directly. We
37:48:15 can just set them equal to something
37:48:16 different. And maybe more importantly
37:48:18 for us, changing a ref doesn't cause a
37:48:22 rerender in React. For reasons that
37:48:24 might not be immediately apparent, refs
37:48:27 tend to commonly be used for accessing
37:48:29 DOM nodes without needing to assign IDs
37:48:32 to them and run DOM manipulation
37:48:34 commands like document.getelement by ID
37:48:37 or query selector. refs give us a way to
37:48:39 access those DOM nodes. Creating a ref
37:48:42 in combination with using a ref prop,
37:48:44 which we're going to see in just a
37:48:45 minute, give us a chance to do some of
37:48:47 those manual DOM manipulation tasks. And
37:48:49 again, the reason for this might not be
37:48:51 immediately apparent, but that's okay.
37:48:53 We're going to get to it. I did mention
37:48:54 this is a very highle overview of refs.
37:48:56 I do dive a little bit more deeply into
37:48:59 refs in my advanced react course which
37:49:01 is kind of the natural progression after
37:49:03 this free intro to react course where in
37:49:06 one of the sections I cover refs in a
37:49:08 little bit more detail and give you a
37:49:09 little bit more practice with them. All
37:49:11 right, why am I talking about all this?
37:49:13 In our chef claude app, we are getting
37:49:16 our recipe. We can click the get a
37:49:17 recipe button. And if we look very
37:49:20 carefully, we'll see that the scroll bar
37:49:22 when the AI finally gets back to us. The
37:49:24 scroll bar on the app will appear and we
37:49:26 can scroll down and see the recipe.
37:49:28 However, as far as the user experience
37:49:30 goes, it's not that great. We don't
37:49:32 really give a clear indication to the
37:49:34 user that the recipe has finally loaded.
37:49:36 And unless their page just happens to be
37:49:39 a little bit longer, like we see here,
37:49:41 in this case, it's a little more
37:49:42 obvious. they can click get a recipe and
37:49:44 what used to be blank will then become
37:49:46 populated with the recipe once it's
37:49:48 loaded. But I don't know that we want to
37:49:50 bank on this because their browser might

39:42:00 and keep accessibility in mind,

39:42:22 focused state, the ability to tab
39:42:25 through them, and you can make divs
39:42:28 accessible, but you just have to do a
39:42:29 lot more work to get that done. However,
39:42:31 when we're working in React and we're
39:42:33 dynamically rendering or removing things
39:42:35 from the page, it's still really
39:42:36 important for us to keep in mind what is
39:42:38 changing and what might be difficult for

40:35:13 And that's the difference between coding

40:35:17 the happy path is when we are writing

40:36:15 cases that can occur when your code is

40:40:04 for the variable that you just created.

40:40:14 colon string here. What TypeScript has
40:40:17 done is it has inferred or derived what
40:40:20 data type my name is supposed to be. Now
40:40:22 while we are about to learn how we can
40:40:24 manually provide different data types to
40:40:26 our variables, I do think it's important
40:40:28 to note that the work that we're about
40:40:30 to do is busy work. It's not something
40:40:32 that TypeScript requires you to do
40:40:34 because it's able to figure out the data
40:40:36 types simply by the data type that you
40:40:38 assign to it. To demonstrate that, if I
40:40:40 then try to say my name equals a number,
40:40:44 we get a red squiggly. And if you hover
40:40:46 your mouse over my name, we'll see that
40:40:48 it says the type of five is not
40:40:50 assignable to the type of string. In
40:40:52 other words, TypeScript is angry that
40:40:54 we're trying to reassign a number value
40:40:57 to a variable that was originally set to
40:41:00 be a string. As a side note, sometimes
40:41:02 people talk about JavaScript's dynamic
40:41:04 typing nature where in regular
40:41:06 JavaScript, this operation is not a
40:41:08 problem at all. They talk about it being
40:41:10 a strength in the flexibility of
40:41:12 JavaScript. But in practice, in the real
40:41:14 world, off the top of my head, I can't
40:41:16 think of a reason why this would be a
40:41:18 beneficial feature for us to be able to
40:41:20 reassign a string value to something
40:41:22 that's a completely different data type.
40:41:24 It's a code smell to me, which means
40:41:26 that if I see something like this or I'm
40:41:28 attempting to do this in my code, then
40:41:30 I'm probably structuring my code in a
40:41:31 bad way. So, TypeScript forces us to
40:41:34 think of better ways to organize our
40:41:35 code. Anyway, let's get back to manual
40:41:37 typing. Now, although TypeScript has
40:41:39 correctly derived that this is going to
40:41:41 be a string type, I can manually give a
40:41:44 type by typing a colon immediately after
40:41:46 my variable name and then typing out the
40:41:48 lowercase version of what data type I
40:41:51 want this to be. So, this might look a
40:41:53 little strange. This is one of those
40:41:54 instances where the TypeScript syntax is
40:41:56 different from JavaScript syntax. This
40:41:58 would not be correct syntax in vanilla
40:42:00 JavaScript, but it is something that
40:42:02 you'll see all throughout TypeScript,
40:42:03 and you'll just get used to typing it.

41:11:55 generic string which would allow that
41:11:58 variable to become any string but
41:12:00 instead it's a literal type an actual
41:12:02 value type of the string Bob with a

41:12:09 will make sense because with let I am
41:12:11 allowed to change the value of this
41:12:14 variable. And Typescript logically
41:12:16 restricts the data type of the value I'm
41:12:18 allowed to change it to to a string. And
41:12:20 with const I'm not allowed to reassign
41:12:23 this value. And so it makes sense for
41:12:25 TypeScript to say this value has to be
41:12:27 the string Bob from here on out. I can't
41:12:30 say my name 2 equals Bill. This not only
41:12:34 makes sense in Typescript but also
41:12:36 JavaScript. We would get warned in

41:12:42 my name too here, it says cannot assign

42:00:03 introduce a bunch of repetitive code.
42:00:05 Now, I did promise earlier that we would
42:00:06 address this issue here where we have
42:00:08 these angle brackets and I think this
42:00:10 would be as good a time as any to jump
42:00:12 into that which is a topic called
42:00:14 generics. So, that's what we'll be
42:00:15 learning about next.
42:00:19 I've mentioned this term generics a
42:00:21 couple times throughout this course and
42:00:23 so it's finally time for us to learn
42:00:24 what generics are. Generics in
42:00:26 Typescript are a really powerful tool
42:00:28 that allow us to add some flexibility to
42:00:31 the types of our existing functions, our
42:00:34 already existing types and other aspects
42:00:36 of TypeScript code. You can kind of
42:00:38 think of it like a function parameter. A
42:00:40 function parameter is a placeholder for
42:00:43 a real value that you use throughout
42:00:45 your function. And a generic is a
42:00:47 placeholder for a type that you can use
42:00:49 throughout your function or whatever
42:00:51 aspect of TypeScript you're using your
42:00:53 generic on. This will make a lot more
42:00:55 sense when we see an example. And
42:00:56 generics use this bracket syntax with
42:00:58 the angle brackets that we've seen a few
42:01:00 times already in our code. Let's see a
42:01:02 bit of a contrived example here. And
42:01:04 then we'll apply what we've learned to

42:02:31 brackets, similar to how we're doing

42:02:38 to be used with this function. A fairly

42:02:43 capital letter T to represent type. But

43:36:58 turns out, whenever I introduce a new

43:37:03 over to you to complete that challenge,
43:37:05 those steps need to happen again. And so
43:37:07 rather than requiring all of our
43:37:09 students to run both npm install and
43:37:12 then npm rundev before they can even

43:37:31 commands as well. This is something I

43:39:51 family for the entire site or change the
43:39:53 background or the foreground color or
43:39:55 anything like that. It's a good place
43:39:56 for us to create CSS variables to do
43:39:59 reset styles like if we want all H1s to
43:40:02 be a specific size and other globally
43:40:05 applied CSS styles like that. One thing

44:12:43 It merits a discussion for us to talk
44:12:45 about how we deal with fonts and images
44:12:47 in Nex.js. Fonts and images are two
44:12:50 topics that in regular web development

44:12:54 optimizations that can happen behind the

44:13:05 just using a regular image element here.

44:13:17 lot of those optimizations and the code

44:13:22 Nex.js. So, the first thing we'll start
44:13:24 with is fonts. If you were poking around

44:13:53 little more detailed into for example

44:14:02 our body font and apply it to all of the
44:14:05 text in the entire body of our project.

44:14:12 alternates font instead. To begin, what
44:14:15 I can do is simply import the name of
44:14:18 the font that I want. And I'm going to
44:14:20 use underscores in place of the spaces.
44:14:22 So the name of our font is Albert Sans.
44:14:25 All I need to do is put an underscore
44:14:26 between those. And I will access the

44:14:47 and say I'm going to save const Albert
44:14:49 Sans and set that equal to the result
44:14:52 from calling the function that we

44:15:00 subset. So I'll say the subsets that I
44:15:03 want to use for this font are the Latin
44:15:05 subset of characters. There's also an
44:15:07 option I'm going to use here that is how
44:15:10 it should be displayed while it's
44:15:12 loading. One of the options if you read
44:15:14 through the documentation is that I can
44:15:16 use this swap value. If you've ever been
44:15:19 on a site that has maybe a bit of a
44:15:21 crazier font and especially if you're on
44:15:23 a slower connection, you might notice
44:15:24 that it loads the fallback font and then
44:15:27 once the intended font actually loads,
44:15:29 then it will change all of the old fonts
44:15:31 for your new font. Swap is a way that
44:15:33 Nex.js JS can do that in a relatively
44:15:35 smooth way that doesn't interrupt too
44:15:37 much the flow of the document or make it
44:15:40 jump around too much or anything like
44:15:41 that. Now, Albert Sands happens to be

44:21:54 The next little housekeeping item that I
44:21:56 wanted to cover is this idea of
44:21:58 optimizing images. Nex.js has some
44:22:00 pretty cool built-in ways to make images
44:22:02 work really well on your website and
44:22:04 allow you to avoid doing any of the
44:22:06 boilerplate optimizations that you do
44:22:09 tend to have to do in any web app that
44:22:11 you're building. As always, if you want
44:22:12 to read a little bit more deeply into
44:22:14 it, you can click on the screenshot here
44:22:16 to go to this page, image optimization
44:22:18 in the Nex.js docs. Let's take a look at
44:22:21 an example. Right here we have our
44:22:23 square hero image. And I did add a
44:22:25 little bit of styling to it because I
44:22:27 noticed that when I made the screen
44:22:29 bigger, the image would kind of float
44:22:31 off to the side. And now it expands a
44:22:33 little bit more to fit its container
44:22:35 better. But that's not the optimization
44:22:37 I wanted to talk about. In Nex.js, there
44:22:39 is a component that you can import
44:22:41 called image and it comes from
44:22:43 next/image and it is an extension on top
44:22:47 of the native img element. So I can just
44:22:50 drop in place the image component from
44:22:52 Nex.js. However, it does have some
44:22:54 required properties that we have to make
44:22:56 sure we include. Of course, it needs to
44:22:58 have a source just like any other image.
44:23:00 It also will send you a warning if you
44:23:02 don't include any kind of alt text. So
44:23:04 we want to make sure we have that. But
44:23:06 it's also required for us to provide
44:23:08 both a width property and a height
44:23:11 property. However, this is where the API
44:23:13 for the image component differs from the
44:23:15 native img element. In the native HTML
44:23:18 img element, when you provide width and
44:23:20 height, it actually sets the width and
44:23:22 height to the sizes that you provide.
44:23:24 The image component from Nex.js doesn't
44:23:27 change the height or width of the image.
44:23:29 What they expect you to provide here is
44:23:31 the native height and width in pixels
44:23:33 for the image that you're displaying. I
44:23:35 believe the original height and width is
44:23:38 742x 742 for this image. So, I would go
44:23:42 ahead and put those in here. Nex.js JS
44:23:44 is not going to force the image to be
44:23:46 this exact size, but it does provide
44:23:48 information for it so that it knows how
44:23:50 to resize it when it needs to and make
44:23:52 sure the proportions stay the same and a
44:23:54 few other optimizations under the hood.
44:23:57 Now, there are other properties that you
44:23:58 can use with the image component and you
44:24:01 can learn more about them from the
44:24:02 documentation, but that's not really
44:24:04 what I want to get into at this point. I
44:24:06 do want to talk about some high-level
44:24:08 optimizations that using the image
44:24:09 component provides. First of all, it
44:24:11 will automatically resize the image so
44:24:14 that it will fit in its container
44:24:15 correctly and make sure that the
44:24:17 proportions stay the same as well as
44:24:19 converting the image file itself to a
44:24:22 more efficient format if the browser
44:24:24 supports it. WEBP, for example, is one
44:24:26 of those more efficient formats that is
44:24:28 that is designed specifically for images
44:24:30 on the web. The image component from
44:24:32 Nex.js JS will also automatically use
44:24:34 lazy loading, which means the image will
44:24:37 only begin to load once that image is
44:24:39 scrolled up into the viewport. This way,
44:24:41 if you have an imageheavy website where
44:24:43 the images are further down on the page,
44:24:45 but it might happen often where the
44:24:47 users don't scroll down to see those
44:24:49 images, but navigate somewhere else or
44:24:51 use some other feature. Nex.js will
44:24:53 avoid the network overhead by not
44:24:55 downloading those images until it's
44:24:57 determined that they're actually needed
44:24:58 in the view. Another benefit of the
44:25:00 image component from Nex.js JS is that
44:25:02 it will help prevent layout shift. This
44:25:04 is when you load a new website, the
44:25:06 images have not yet loaded, but all of
44:25:08 the text or other elements has loaded
44:25:10 and then only after the image begins to
44:25:12 load does the text shift down into a
44:25:14 different place. X.js will reserve a
44:25:17 space on your page for the size of the
44:25:19 image that is still incoming so that
44:25:21 there isn't that jank of things shifting
44:25:24 around the page. So, the image component
44:25:26 from Nex.js is a really great component
44:25:28 to use. However, I do have a little bit
44:25:31 of sad news. As it turns out, the web
44:25:33 container system that Scribba runs on,
44:25:35 which allows us to run back-end code in
44:25:38 a browser, unfortunately, this image
44:25:40 component simply doesn't work in that
44:25:42 web container system. That said, there
44:25:44 is another minor optimization that we
44:25:46 can include. I'm going to go ahead and
44:25:47 change this back to img. I'm going to
44:25:50 take out the height and width here, and
44:25:52 we no longer need this import.

44:32:11 to the about route. And clicking home,
44:32:13 of course, takes me to the homepage.
44:32:15 However, there is something pretty
44:32:16 inefficient happening here. If we look
44:32:18 very closely, we might see a little
44:32:20 flash of the content. Of course, once
44:32:22 it's cached, it's really not too bad,
44:32:24 but it's kind of a slow transition from
44:32:26 one page to the next. That's because it
44:32:29 is doing a full page refresh every time
44:32:31 we navigate to a new route. A href is
44:32:34 taking the browser to the slabout route.
44:32:37 That is making a request out to the
44:32:39 server to get whatever information is
44:32:41 supposed to come back when the URL is at
44:32:43 slabout. The server, Nex.js JS in this
44:32:46 case knows that /about is its own page
44:32:49 or route and so it sends back the full
44:32:52 HTML that needs to be loaded into the
44:32:54 browser. Fortunately, Nex.js has
44:32:56 included a way for us to do this in a
44:32:58 little bit more of an efficient manner.
44:33:00 You can click on the screenshot that you
44:33:02 see here to go to the linking and
44:33:04 navigating page in the Nex.js
44:33:05 documentation. And we can see on that
44:33:07 page that there are four ways to
44:33:09 navigate between routes in Nex.js. The
44:33:11 one that we are going to concern
44:33:13 ourselves with at this point is using
44:33:15 the link component like it says here.
44:33:17 Fortunately, the link component is
44:33:19 extremely easy to use. We need to import
44:33:21 link from next slashlink and then the
44:33:25 basic API for it works exactly like an
44:33:28 anchor tag. So, we can simply replace
44:33:30 our anchors with this new link
44:33:33 component. Just like that. Now, let's
44:33:35 see what happens. I'll hit save and
44:33:37 we'll click on about. And it might have
44:33:39 been a little bit difficult to notice,
44:33:40 but it immediately transferred us to the
44:33:43 about page. I can go back and forth
44:33:45 between home and about, and it's pretty
44:33:47 fast. The reason the behavior has
44:33:49 changed is because now we're using the
44:33:51 link component. And when you use the
44:33:52 link component, it enables clientside
44:33:55 navigation in Nex.js. That means we're
44:33:57 no longer needing a roundtrip to the
44:34:00 server in order to get the content for
44:34:01 the new page that we're routing to.
44:34:03 Another neat thing that using the link
44:34:05 component does is that Nex.js JS will
44:34:07 actually prefetch the upcoming route
44:34:10 whenever that link is in view on the web
44:34:12 page. So if you can imagine having a
44:34:14 long page that we need to scroll down
44:34:16 to, maybe way down here below there's a
44:34:18 link, as soon as that link becomes
44:34:20 visible, Nex.js will prefetch that route
44:34:23 in the background so that by the time
44:34:24 the user actually clicks that link, it
44:34:27 will already have been loaded in the
44:34:28 background in the browser. That is the
44:34:30 default behavior for a link. Although,
44:34:32 if there was maybe some really expensive
44:34:34 route that the users hardly ever click,
44:34:37 you may decide to improve the
44:34:38 performance by manually turning the
44:34:40 prefetch prop set to false on the link
44:34:43 element. It'll be a little bit difficult
44:34:45 to actually demonstrate that here, and
44:34:47 it's not really something you'll find
44:34:49 yourself using super often, so I'm going
44:34:51 to go ahead and leave that off. Another
44:34:53 benefit that we get from using the link
44:34:55 component is that it will maintain React
44:34:57 state between the navigations. You can
44:34:59 imagine if we have some kind of React
44:35:01 state in let's say the navbar of our
44:35:03 project. Maybe it's maintaining a
44:35:05 loggedin status for the current user so
44:35:07 that it can display their username up in
44:35:09 the navigation bar. If we were
44:35:10 performing a full round trip to the
44:35:12 server and doing a refresh of the page
44:35:14 by using an anchor tag, for example, to
44:35:16 navigate from one route to the next, our
44:35:18 navbar would have to reestablish that
44:35:20 state. However, because we're doing
44:35:22 clientside navigation here, we no longer
44:35:25 have to worry about that. the state will
44:35:26 be maintained because our site is not
44:35:28 being refreshed. So as a quick recap, we
44:35:31 need to import link from next/link and
44:35:33 then anywhere we want to add a link, we
44:35:36 simply use this link component. And we
44:35:38 need to make sure we specify an href to
44:35:41 designate where that link should take
44:35:42 the user. Easy enough. Let's get back to

44:42:38 We're about ready to create our 3D
44:42:40 models page and load the data from a
44:42:43 fake source for now. But in order for
44:42:45 the way that we're going to be fetching
44:42:46 that data to make sense, we first need
44:42:49 to have a quick discussion about client
44:42:51 versus server components. This is not
44:42:53 going to be an in-depth look into those,
44:42:55 at least not quite yet, but rather we'll
44:42:57 just be enough information for us to
44:43:00 make sense of what we're about to see
44:43:02 with how we fetch data in Nex.js.
44:43:04 Assuming you're coming to this course
44:43:06 with a pretty strong familiarity with
44:43:08 clientside React, you should already be
44:43:10 familiar with the paradigm that you have
44:43:12 to use when you're dealing with fetching
44:43:14 data in client components. You see, the
44:43:16 code for client components is only ever
44:43:19 run in the browser. The server will send
44:43:21 the JavaScript bundle to the browser and
44:43:23 then the browser will sort of unpack it
44:43:25 and actually run the code in that
44:43:28 bundle. This means when you load a new
44:43:30 page in React, React will first mount
44:43:32 the component to the page and then
44:43:35 initiate any fetch requests you have.
44:43:37 Let's say you're on a slow connection or
44:43:39 the server that is responding to your
44:43:42 fetch request is running kind of slow.
44:43:44 Well, your user will have navigated to
44:43:46 the new page but won't be looking at any
44:43:49 of the data they were expecting to see.
44:43:51 Best case scenario, you have some kind
44:43:52 of loading spinner that looks pretty
44:43:54 good and users are kind of used to that
44:43:56 anyway. However, as the developer
44:43:58 creating this, you need to work outside
44:44:00 of the regular paradigm of React by
44:44:03 including use effects and saving the
44:44:05 data that comes in inside of React state
44:44:07 because you're dealing with the
44:44:09 asynchronous nature of data fetching in
44:44:11 general. Again, assuming you're coming
44:44:13 from, for example, one of my React
44:44:15 courses here on Scribba, this is
44:44:17 something that you should feel pretty
44:44:18 familiar with. A relatively new paradigm
44:44:20 for React, however, is the ability for
44:44:22 us to use something called server
44:44:25 components. A key aspect of server
44:44:27 components is that the code is run on
44:44:29 the server. Now, I did put here that the
44:44:31 code is only run on the server. That's
44:44:34 actually not strictly true, but it helps
44:44:36 get the point across. Because the
44:44:38 component is rendered on the server, it
44:44:40 can perform any data fetching before it
44:44:42 does the rendering. Whereas in the
44:44:44 client component, it would mount and
44:44:46 render the component first, then kick
44:44:48 off the fetch request. In server
44:44:50 components, we can fetch the data and
44:44:52 wait for it to come back before we even
44:44:54 begin the rendering process. This means
44:44:56 we don't have to use use effect or use
44:44:59 state in order to deal with the
44:45:01 asynchronous nature of data fetching. We
44:45:03 can simply await the data and then use
44:45:05 it in the markup. What we return from
44:45:07 our React component will include the
44:45:09 data that we fetched. The code for doing
44:45:11 this is extraordinarily simple, at least
44:45:14 compared to what you had to do in
44:45:16 clientside React using effect and use
44:45:18 state. Let's see what that looks like.
44:45:20 I've added a new route to our little
44:45:22 sample app here that is called posts.
44:45:25 And we're going to use the JSON
44:45:26 placeholder API to just really quickly
44:45:28 grab some sample data. In client side
44:45:31 React, I would have to create new state
44:45:34 to hold the data that comes back. I
44:45:36 would need to add a use effect to fetch
44:45:39 the data. Once the data comes back, I
44:45:41 would set it into state. Changing state
44:45:43 would rerender the component now with
44:45:45 the state holding the data from the API.
44:45:47 And that's when the user would finally
44:45:49 see the data on the page. I'm just
44:45:51 writing comments for this, but if I were
44:45:53 writing the real code, it would add an
44:45:55 additional maybe seven to 10 lines, if
44:45:58 not more if I was dealing with loading
44:46:00 states and errors and so forth. With
44:46:02 server components in Nex.js, this
44:46:04 becomes significantly simpler. I have
44:46:06 the ability to change my component into
44:46:08 an async function, which means I can
44:46:11 just say response is going to be what
44:46:13 comes back when I await a fetch request
44:46:16 to the JSON placeholder API. Then I can
44:46:19 await to get the data. So I'll say cons
44:46:23 data equals await res.json. And treating
44:46:26 this like it's synchronous code, I can
44:46:28 now just return the code that I want
44:46:30 with the data included. Let's format it
44:46:32 a little bit. I'm going to use a pre-tag
44:46:36 here and I will just JSON stringify the
44:46:40 data that came back and maybe we'll
44:46:42 format it a little bit nice. Okay, I'll
44:46:45 hit save. I'm on the about route. Let's
44:46:47 go over to posts and check it out. We
44:46:50 have the data there in our app. Now, our
44:46:52 style is a little bit broken because it
44:46:54 is overflowing, but uh we'll just sweep
44:46:57 that under the rug for now. There is a
44:46:59 lot more to understand when it comes to
44:47:01 server components. We may touch on it
44:47:03 again later in the course. For now, what
44:47:05 we've talked about here is good enough
44:47:07 for us to continue onward. However, if
44:47:09 you do want to really dive deep into
44:47:11 understanding server components, how
44:47:13 they're different from client
44:47:14 components, and how they're different
44:47:16 from just plain server side rendering in
44:47:18 React, I'd like to give a shout out to
44:47:20 Josh Ko's article here called Making
44:47:23 Sense of Server Components. It's kind of
44:47:25 a long read. It might take a few times
44:47:26 through for it to start making sense,
44:47:28 but it's very well explained. So feel
44:47:30 free to click on the screenshot here to
44:47:32 go over to this blog post. One last
44:47:34 thing that I think is important to point
44:47:36 out is that because this code is only
44:47:38 run on the server, it's okay for us to
44:47:40 put things like sensitive API keys
44:47:43 inside of our fetch requests here. Well,
44:47:45 I should put a caveat on that. What I
44:47:47 mean is that someone using your site
44:47:49 will not be able to inspect the browser
44:47:51 code and see your API keys anywhere
44:47:54 because this fetch request is happening
44:47:56 on the server before the code ever gets
44:47:58 to the browser. If on the other hand, if
44:48:00 you were checking this code into a Git
44:48:02 repository and posting it online on
44:48:04 GitHub, well then you'd have another
44:48:05 problem. You would want to use
44:48:07 environment variables and so forth. I
44:48:09 just don't want anyone to take it as
44:48:11 gospel that I said you're completely
44:48:12 safe to use API keys here. In fact, not
44:48:15 only can you use API keys here, but
44:48:17 because this is happening in a server
44:48:19 environment, you can use an OM like
44:48:21 Prisma to directly fetch data from a
44:48:24 database right here inside of your
44:48:26 components. In the end, it really
44:48:28 simplifies the paradigm when it comes to
44:48:30 fetching data inside of your React code.

44:55:30 brings us to our topic which is dynamic
44:55:32 routes. And although it sounds really
44:55:34 philosophical, dynamic routes are used
44:55:36 when you don't know the path of your URL
44:55:39 ahead of time. You can read up on
44:55:40 dynamic routes from the Nex.js docs if
44:55:42 you'd like. Just click on the screenshot

44:55:48 you don't know the exact segment names

44:55:51 routes from dynamic data, you can use

44:55:56 are actually pretty easy to create. The
44:55:58 first thing you need to do is create a
44:55:59 folder just like when you're creating
44:56:01 any other route whose name represents
44:56:03 what you want the path to contain. So
44:56:05 for example, you might want the segment
44:56:07 of your path to be the ID of the item.
44:56:10 Or if we had this with our blog posts,
44:56:12 it might be something like the slug,

44:56:15 post with dashes in between, all
44:56:17 lowercase. I think you know what I'm

45:47:39 In the next one, we will have a

45:47:42 the active link.
45:47:46 Let's talk just a little bit more about
45:47:48 this use client directive. And that's
45:47:50 actually what it's called when you have
45:47:52 this text at the top of your page. It's
45:47:54 called a directive in JavaScript. If
45:47:56 you've been around web development for a
45:47:58 little while, you might recognize
45:47:59 another directive that was really
45:48:01 popular to use in vanilla JavaScript
45:48:03 called use strict. Essentially, it's a
45:48:05 way to direct how the rest of that file
45:48:08 should be treated. That's a really
45:48:10 simplistic way of putting it, but that's
45:48:12 good enough for now. Now, I showed you
45:48:13 this screenshot before, which goes to
45:48:15 the useclient documentation in Nex.js,
45:48:18 but I failed to mention that useclient
45:48:20 is actually not a Nex.js thing. It's a
45:48:23 React thing. Here's the same
45:48:25 documentation for use client in React.
45:48:27 It just so happens that Nex.js tends to
45:48:29 implement some of the most cutting edge
45:48:31 features of React. And in fact, Nex.js
45:48:34 JS has been using client components with
45:48:36 use client for a bit longer than React
45:48:39 has even had it in its main release of
45:48:42 React 19. Nex.js tends to use what's
45:48:45 called a Canary version of React which
45:48:47 has some of the most cutting edge
45:48:49 features that are going to be coming out
45:48:50 in the major releases of React in the
45:48:52 future. But as of the time of recording,
45:48:54 it's officially accepted in both React
45:48:57 and implemented in Nex.js. So feel free
45:49:00 to click on the link that you see here
45:49:01 to go to the React documentation for use
45:49:04 client and in that documentation it will
45:49:06 talk a little bit about the client
45:49:08 boundary. Now I actually first heard
45:49:10 about the client boundary from Josh Ko's
45:49:12 article called making sense of react
45:49:14 server components and it's a bit of a
45:49:16 lengthy article but but is a really good
45:49:18 one to read if you want to dive a little
45:49:20 bit deeper into what React server
45:49:22 components are. It's important to know
45:49:24 that in Nex.js JS all components are by
45:49:27 default server components. In other
45:49:29 words, anytime you import a component
45:49:31 that you created or are creating a page,
45:49:34 if you don't include the use client
45:49:37 directive, then it will be considered a
45:49:39 server component, which means the code
45:49:41 for that component will be run and
45:49:43 rendered on the server and then sent
45:49:46 down as markup to the client or to the
45:49:48 browser. Alternatively, you can mark a
45:49:51 component with the use client directive.
45:49:53 And that not only will mark that
45:49:55 component, in this case, the layout
45:49:57 component as a client component, which
45:50:00 means that the code for that component
45:50:02 won't be run on the server, but instead
45:50:04 will be sent down to the client or the
45:50:07 browser for the browser to run that
45:50:09 code. But a crucial thing to understand
45:50:11 is that when you include use client
45:50:14 inside of a component like this, it
45:50:16 creates a boundary around itself and all
45:50:19 of its children that it imports and
45:50:21 renders. In this sense, it creates a
45:50:24 boundary like you might see here where
45:50:26 all of the code below use client
45:50:28 implicitly turns into client components
45:50:31 and are no longer run on the server, but
45:50:34 instead are sent down to the browser for
45:50:36 the browser to run it. So in the case of
45:50:38 what we're trying to do where we're
45:50:40 trying to style the active link and
45:50:42 really what we care about the most is
45:50:44 inside the navbar, we'll likely find
45:50:46 that it is overkill to change the entire
45:50:49 layout where the navbar is nested inside
45:50:51 of into a client component, but instead
45:50:54 to just change the navbar into a client
45:50:57 component. By doing this, it creates a
45:50:59 client boundary, but only around the
45:51:01 navbar. And this way, the server can
45:51:03 still be in charge of rendering a
45:51:05 majority of the layout here. Now, there
45:51:07 are definitely trade-offs to this, and
45:51:09 it's important to note, as I mentioned
45:51:11 before, turning something into a client
45:51:13 component is not a bad thing, but it can
45:51:16 be easy to overdo it, to make too many
45:51:18 things into client components
45:51:20 unnecessarily. So, coming back to our
45:51:22 active style link, we have a few
45:51:24 options. The first one, which we already
45:51:26 talked about, probably isn't the
45:51:27 greatest idea. We could change the root
45:51:30 layout into a client component, which
45:51:31 effectively would turn our entire Nex.js
45:51:34 app into client components. In our case,
45:51:37 if we were to come to the layout file,
45:51:39 we can see that we have separated our
45:51:41 navbar into its own component. But if
45:51:44 instead we had kept all of the code from
45:51:47 the navbar here in the layout, you could
45:51:49 see how it might be tempting to change
45:51:51 this into a client component so that we
45:51:54 could use the use pathname hook so that
45:51:57 we could use the current path name to
45:52:00 determine which of the links in the
45:52:02 navbar is the current active link. But
45:52:05 as you can imagine, doing this in the
45:52:06 root layout is a bit overkill and we
45:52:09 have an opportunity to access a smaller
45:52:12 scope of components to turn into client
45:52:14 components. So two other options we
45:52:16 have, both of which I think could
45:52:18 legitimately be argued for. One is to
45:52:21 change our navbar into a client
45:52:23 component, which means when we're
45:52:25 loading our app, all of the content of
45:52:27 the pages, we go to 3D models just to
45:52:30 get a little bit more content on here.
45:52:32 Everything that we see on the page would
45:52:34 stay a server component except for this
45:52:38 little navigation bar at the top. And to
45:52:40 be clear, right now we're just talking
45:52:42 about the full site navigation, not the
45:52:44 categories navigation. Not yet, at
45:52:46 least. Alternatively, we could instead
45:52:48 of changing the whole navbar into a
45:52:50 client component, we could change just
45:52:52 the individual nav link into a client
45:52:54 component. The nav link component is an
45:52:57 even smaller scope than the navbar since
45:52:59 the navbar renders two nav links. There
45:53:02 are some ramifications for doing one or
45:53:04 the other and it's honestly a little bit
45:53:06 outside the scope of what I'm planning
45:53:09 on talking about here. So, for our
45:53:11 purposes, there are some trade-offs
45:53:13 between choosing to make the navbar or
45:53:15 the nav link into a client component.
45:53:18 And I'm actually going to give that to
45:53:20 you as the first part of your challenge.
45:53:22 I'll be honest and say there isn't
45:53:23 necessarily a right or wrong answer
45:53:26 here, but I did want to give you a
45:53:27 chance to stop listening to my voice and
45:53:30 to start listening to the voice in your
45:53:31 head. Think about which of those
45:53:34 components, the nav link or the navbar
45:53:36 would be a better option in your mind
45:53:39 for changing into a client component and
45:53:42 also start thinking through the
45:53:43 logistic. If you change the navbar into
45:53:45 the client component, how will that
45:53:47 change your navbar code in order to
45:53:49 style the active link? If you choose to
45:53:51 change the nav link into a client
45:53:53 component, what will that code look
45:53:55 like? And because we're here in Scrumba,

46:00:16 All right, I have a little bit of a
46:00:18 confession to make. When I was giving
46:00:19 you different options for which
46:00:21 components to turn into client
46:00:23 components in order to use the use
46:00:25 pathname hook, I mentioned that we could
46:00:27 change the root layout into a client
46:00:29 component. And I mentioned that it
46:00:31 effectively turns the whole app into a
46:00:33 client component. Well, I have a
46:00:34 confession to make. That's actually not
46:00:36 entirely true, and we'll talk about why
46:00:38 in a second. If it were true, this is
46:00:40 essentially what we would be talking
46:00:41 about. If we were to turn the layout for
46:00:44 our 3D models page in Printforge into a
46:00:47 client component, I was giving you the
46:00:48 impression that doing so would turn the
46:00:50 entire thing into a client component,
46:00:52 even the children that were being
46:00:54 rendered inside of the page component
46:00:56 that Nex.js renders. And I made it seem
46:00:58 like if we just make the little
46:01:00 navigation bar into the client
46:01:02 component, then it would allow the rest
46:01:04 of this to still be server rendered. So,
46:01:06 I'm sorry that I oversimplified this,
46:01:08 but now is the time for us to clarify
46:01:10 exactly what's happening with client
46:01:12 components. Essentially, there are two
46:01:14 ways to turn something into a client
46:01:15 component. The first one is the most
46:01:17 obvious. It's any file that you mark
46:01:19 with use client. The component that's
46:01:21 being exported from a file with the use
46:01:24 client directive at the top will
46:01:25 automatically be turned into a client
46:01:27 component. That's something that comes
46:01:29 from React. Another way that components
46:01:31 get turned into client components is if
46:01:34 they are imported and rendered by
46:01:36 another client component. Or in other
46:01:38 words, any file that has the use client
46:01:40 directive at the top. If it also imports
46:01:43 components from elsewhere and then
46:01:44 renders them by name in the return of
46:01:47 that client component, those imported
46:01:49 and rendered components will
46:01:50 automatically be turned into client
46:01:52 components. And that continues on down
46:01:55 the chain. If a component is turned into
46:01:57 a client component because it was
46:01:59 imported and rendered by another
46:02:00 component using use client and then that
46:02:03 component also imports and renders other
46:02:06 components in its content. Those
46:02:07 components will also be turned into
46:02:09 client components. Let's look at our
46:02:11 simplified codebase here for a second.
46:02:13 We had changed the layout into a client
46:02:15 component. And what I had been saying
46:02:17 before is that any of the components
46:02:19 that are being rendered by this layout
46:02:21 component or root layout here would
46:02:23 automatically get turned into client
46:02:25 components. But it actually is a bit
46:02:28 more nuanced than that. And I wanted to
46:02:30 demonstrate this with kind of a cool
46:02:31 little trick. So I'm going to get rid of
46:02:33 that console log. And I'm going to go
46:02:36 ahead and add a console log that says
46:02:40 this is the root layout component. And
46:02:43 then I'm going to use a little trick
46:02:44 that allows me to determine if the
46:02:47 current component is running in the
46:02:49 server or in the client. You see, when
46:02:51 JavaScript is running in the server, you
46:02:54 have access to global variables like
46:02:56 module, for example. And when you're
46:02:58 running code inside of a browser, you
46:03:01 have access to global variables like
46:03:03 window or document. Now, if I try to
46:03:07 console log something like window from a
46:03:09 server component, it's going to display
46:03:12 undefined. It's not going to know what
46:03:14 window is. Same with if I were in client
46:03:16 side JavaScript and I tried to access
46:03:18 module, it's going to be undefined
46:03:20 because that global variable doesn't
46:03:22 exist in the context of the browser. So,
46:03:24 something I can do is I can check if the
46:03:27 type of window is equal to undefined,
46:03:30 then I know that I am currently in the
46:03:32 context of a server. And so what I can
46:03:35 do is console log this is a server and
46:03:38 I'll just add the word component on the
46:03:40 back here. And otherwise this will be
46:03:43 considered a client component. And uh

46:20:11 All right, let's dive into what we are
46:20:13 essentially going to be covering for the
46:20:14 rest of this section, and that has to do
46:20:16 with different rendering strategies that
46:20:18 Nex.js uses to try and optimize the
46:20:21 application that you're building. Now,
46:20:23 I'll be honest. This first lesson will
46:20:24 be a little bit slideheavy and is going
46:20:26 to include some new terminology that you
46:20:28 may not be familiar with. I have worked
46:20:30 really hard to try and break down these
46:20:33 different concepts so that they are as
46:20:35 approachable and understandable as
46:20:37 possible. Mostly because when I started
46:20:39 learning it, it wasn't immediately
46:20:41 intuitive. And it really just took, as
46:20:43 always, some extra practice and exposure
46:20:46 in order to understand what was going
46:20:48 on. I want to fasttrack that for you so
46:20:50 that you can just jump right into
46:20:51 Next.js. JS understand these different
46:20:53 rendering strategies and be able to hit
46:20:55 the ground running with writing your
46:20:57 code. And a bit of a spoiler alert, it
46:20:59 turns out Nex.js will actually handle a
46:21:01 lot of this for you and you get to worry
46:21:03 just about the code that you're writing
46:21:05 instead. That will make a little more
46:21:07 sense as we go on. All right, what do I
46:21:09 mean by rendering strategies? Well, the
46:21:11 whole concept of it is to give your app
46:21:13 the ability to decide when and where a
46:21:16 page's HTML is built. At the end of the
46:21:19 day, when all is said and done, your
46:21:21 application's main job is to compile
46:21:23 HTML and send it to the browser.
46:21:26 Obviously, there's a lot more to it than
46:21:27 that, especially when you take into
46:21:29 account the interactivity and all those
46:21:31 extra things that come with having a
46:21:33 robust Nex.js application, but if you
46:21:36 were just to look at it from a highlevel
46:21:38 overview, the purpose of your app is to
46:21:40 render HTML and send it to the browser.
46:21:42 The different rendering strategies that
46:21:44 Nex.js uses help Next.js JS decide when
46:21:47 and where that happens. And I would say
46:21:49 there are three main places and times
46:21:50 that this happens. The first one is
46:21:53 called build time. When you're writing
46:21:55 your Nex.js code, you're typically doing
46:21:57 it inside of a development environment.
46:21:59 When we're running npm rundev and we get
46:22:02 what's called hot module reloading,
46:22:04 meaning every time you save your code,
46:22:06 your local version of the app running on
46:22:08 say localhost 3000 will automatically
46:22:11 update. And that's great for when you're
46:22:13 developing your code, actually building
46:22:15 the project. But when it comes time to
46:22:17 deploy that to a live website, you have
46:22:19 to go through what's called a build
46:22:21 process. This process will take all of
46:22:23 your code and kind of simplify it down.
46:22:26 It will shake off any of the things you
46:22:28 don't actually need. In the case of
46:22:29 using TypeScript, it will convert all of
46:22:31 the TypeScript into plain JavaScript
46:22:33 that a server and browser can understand
46:22:36 and do a bunch of other optimizations so
46:22:38 that when you deploy it online, it will
46:22:40 just work a lot easier for everybody
46:22:42 around the world accessing your app. The
46:22:44 key thing to know about the buildtime
46:22:46 process is that it usually happens one
46:22:48 time right before you push your code to
46:22:50 some server somewhere that will serve it
46:22:52 up to the world. Any changes that you
46:22:54 make to your code won't just
46:22:56 automatically refresh your site
46:22:58 everywhere around the world. You'll have
46:22:59 to do another build process and then
46:23:02 push that new code live up to the
46:23:04 internet. When the HTML is built at
46:23:06 build time only, it means that it gets
46:23:08 pre-rendered once and then every time
46:23:10 somebody comes to that website, it is
46:23:12 served from a cache or like a memory of
46:23:14 that HTML file. By the way, we'll be
46:23:17 talking in depth more about each one of
46:23:18 these later. Another time that your HTML
46:23:20 can be built is at request time. This
46:23:23 means that the HTML gets rendered on
46:23:25 demand and it will always be fresh. This
46:23:27 would be for example when somebody tries
46:23:29 to get to one of the routes in your
46:23:31 application and that route has not been
46:23:33 pre-rendered at build time, but instead
46:23:35 the server then immediately upon
46:23:38 receiving the request builds the HTML
46:23:40 page and sends it down to the browser.
46:23:42 So that's what this means by request
46:23:44 time. It's kind of happening on the fly
46:23:46 right as somebody is requesting it. And
46:23:48 lastly, in terms of a clientsideon
46:23:51 application, sometimes the building of
46:23:53 the HTML will actually happen in the
46:23:55 browser. So this is when the HTML is
46:23:57 built in the browser after your
46:23:59 JavaScript loads. For those of you that
46:24:01 have done front-end only React, this is
46:24:03 probably what you're most used to.
46:24:05 Wherever your React application is being
46:24:07 served from, it sends down the entire
46:24:10 React application, but really very
46:24:12 minimal HTML. And then once the entire
46:24:15 React application loads in your browser,
46:24:17 your browser is then in charge of
46:24:19 building all of the HTML and putting it
46:24:21 into the DOM. This in browser rendering
46:24:23 is not something we're going to be
46:24:25 covering a whole lot in this lesson
46:24:27 because the default with Nex.js is to
46:24:30 use server components and especially on
46:24:32 the route level. Entire routes or pages
46:24:35 are almost always going to be rendered
46:24:37 on the server in Nex.js. There's some
46:24:39 little exceptions to that that we're
46:24:41 going to talk about in a little bit. So
46:24:43 let's look at these different rendering
46:24:44 strategies. And the ones that we'll be
46:24:46 talking about by name are static site
46:24:48 generation, which is oftentimes referred
46:24:50 to as SSG. Then there's incremental
46:24:53 static regeneration, which is kind of a
46:24:55 sibling to static site generation. And
46:24:57 this is often referred to as ISR. Again,
46:24:59 please don't let these scary terms throw
46:25:01 you off. We are going to be talking
46:25:02 about them and hopefully ideally going
46:25:04 to make them as approachable as
46:25:06 possible. And lastly, there is
46:25:08 serverside rendering or sometimes called
46:25:10 dynamic rendering. And this is usually
46:25:12 referred to as SSR. So with static site
46:25:15 generation, when does it get rendered?
46:25:16 It gets rendered just one time at build
46:25:19 time. This is again when you run npm run
46:25:21 build and npm start. At least
46:25:24 specifically with Nex.js. Those are the
46:25:25 commands that you would run to build and
46:25:27 run the built version of your site. Now
46:25:29 you can build your site locally, but
46:25:31 once you build it and run the built
46:25:33 version, if you make changes to your
46:25:35 code, you are not going to see changes
46:25:36 to the built version. You would have to
46:25:38 run it again to see the changes that you
46:25:40 made. So where is it rendered? Actually
46:25:42 the answer is basically going to be the
46:25:44 same for all of these. They are rendered
46:25:45 on the server. And in this case it's
46:25:47 when next build or if you're using a
46:25:49 package.json with a script that uses npm
46:25:52 run build. Uh maybe to clarify that if I
46:25:54 come to package.json you can see that
46:25:56 running npm run build right here under
46:26:00 the hood it really just runs next build.
46:26:02 So those are completely interchangeable.
46:26:03 In this case incremental static
46:26:05 regeneration ISR is just a little bit
46:26:07 different. It gets rendered once at
46:26:09 buildtime, but then certain pages or
46:26:11 parts of a page can be regenerated after
46:26:14 a specified time window. Now, I have a
46:26:16 little asterisk here for once at
46:26:18 buildtime because it really depends on
46:26:19 your code. And at this time, we're not
46:26:21 going to jump too deeply into that, but
46:26:23 it's enough to know that by default,
46:26:25 it's going to generate the page once at
46:26:27 build time, just like the static site
46:26:28 generation did. But then you specify a
46:26:31 sort of refresh time where after a
46:26:33 certain amount of time passes, it will
46:26:35 regenerate that page so that it can be
46:26:37 at least a little bit updated. Once
46:26:39 again, this is going to be rendered on
46:26:40 the server and it will happen in the
46:26:42 background whenever a request triggers
46:26:44 that regeneration after the time window.
46:26:47 Again, at this time, a highle overview
46:26:49 is good enough. If this doesn't make
46:26:50 sense, that's okay. We'll see examples
46:26:52 of it. And lastly, with serverside
46:26:53 rendering, it will be rendered on every
46:26:55 request from the browser. It's still
46:26:57 rendered on the server. however,
46:26:59 whenever a request comes for that page.

46:27:03 all at once. I do still have more slides

46:27:40 Okay, so we've talked about some of the
46:27:42 rendering strategies that are used by
46:27:44 Nex.js and I thought it might be helpful
46:27:46 to look at what I'm calling a decision
46:27:48 tree. Now, I put a little asterisk by
46:27:50 the word decision. We're going to talk
46:27:52 about that after we see the diagram. I
46:27:54 think a helpful approach to
46:27:55 understanding these rendering strategies
46:27:57 is to ask yourself, is the page content
46:28:00 going to be identical for everyone? For
46:28:02 example, if you come to a homepage or an
46:28:05 about page, it's a lot less likely that
46:28:08 person A is going to see content that's
46:28:10 different from the content you would see
46:28:12 for person B. Unless, of course, you're
46:28:14 talking about maybe the little avatar in
46:28:16 the upper right with their name or
46:28:17 something if they're logged in. But in
46:28:19 this case, I'm just talking about the
46:28:21 page content itself. Is it going to be
46:28:23 identical for everyone? If the answer is
46:28:25 yes, then you can come to another
46:28:27 question where is it okay for this
46:28:28 content to be outdated? Think again in
46:28:31 terms of an about page. Is it okay if a
46:28:34 user comes to our site and there's a
46:28:35 little bit of old content there, at
46:28:37 least for a little while? If that's
46:28:38 true, then you could consider using
46:28:40 static site generation. This means that
46:28:42 the content you put on your about page
46:28:44 will be generated once when the whole
46:28:46 app is built. And that content is pretty
46:28:49 unlikely to change unless you explicitly
46:28:51 go into the code or the content for that
46:28:54 page and make changes. Making changes to
46:28:56 a page that was generated statically
46:28:59 means that in order to see those changes
46:29:01 live, you would have to rebuild the
46:29:02 entire site and deploy it again live.
46:29:05 Now, I kind of make that sound like it's
46:29:07 a scary process. Oftentimes it is as
46:29:10 simple as merging maybe a feature branch
46:29:12 to master and pushing that to GitHub and
46:29:14 then you would have an automated process
46:29:16 that notices a new commit to the master
46:29:18 branch and might automatically rebuild
46:29:20 the live version of the site and push it
46:29:22 out everywhere that's seeing it. But
46:29:24 still for large sites that can be a bit
46:29:26 of a process. So if it's okay that the
46:29:28 page content that's the same for
46:29:30 everyone is a little bit outdated then
46:29:32 you might use static site generation. If
46:29:34 on the other hand you want that
46:29:35 information to periodically update, you
46:29:38 might say, "No, I don't really want it
46:29:40 to be very outdated." In which case, you
46:29:42 might use incremental static
46:29:43 regeneration. We'll get a chance to see
46:29:45 this in action. And honestly, I think
46:29:47 this might be the most difficult to
46:29:49 understand, but don't worry, it's still
46:29:51 not that difficult. The idea behind
46:29:52 incremental static regeneration is that
46:29:55 the page will originally be statically
46:29:57 generated just like we saw up here. This
46:29:59 will happen at build time. However, we
46:30:01 will also specify an amount of time that
46:30:04 we are okay with that information being
46:30:06 stale. And that time window might be as
46:30:09 little as maybe a minute or two, which
46:30:11 honestly I think is generally frowned
46:30:13 upon, or I think more commonly is along
46:30:15 the lines of an hour or a day or a week
46:30:18 even. An example of this might be
46:30:20 something like a new arrivals page for
46:30:23 maybe listing products or in terms of
46:30:25 our Print Forge app, maybe new arrivals
46:30:27 of 3D models that were recently uploaded
46:30:29 to the site. It would generally be okay
46:30:31 if somebody came to the site and didn't
46:30:33 have the absolute most up-to-date list
46:30:35 of new arrivals of those 3D models, but
46:30:38 we also would want them every 30 or 60
46:30:40 minutes or maybe even a little more to
46:30:42 update so that when somebody comes to
46:30:44 the site, they're still seeing something
46:30:46 that's relatively new. We don't want it
46:30:48 to be where if the last build that we
46:30:50 ran was maybe 30 days ago that they
46:30:52 never get to see new information here.
46:30:54 So we would specify how long we're
46:30:56 allowed to allow that information to be
46:30:58 stale. And now this is the important
46:31:01 part to understand. If a new request
46:31:03 comes in for the page that is using ISR
46:31:06 and that stale window of say 30 minutes
46:31:08 has passed, maybe 31 minutes since the
46:31:11 last time it was built, it will first
46:31:14 serve a stale version to the person that
46:31:16 just visited the page. And behind the
46:31:18 scenes, it will regenerate the page so
46:31:20 that the next person who comes to this
46:31:22 page will get an updated version. and
46:31:24 then it will kind of restart that 30
46:31:25 minute timer. If that definition went
46:31:28 over your head, it's okay. We're going
46:31:29 to see an example in the code and I
46:31:31 think that will help solidify the
46:31:32 concept here. All right, now let's go
46:31:34 back to the original question. Is the
46:31:36 page content going to be identical for
46:31:38 everyone? Obviously, in dynamic web
46:31:40 apps, this oftentimes isn't the case.
46:31:42 You will want to display data that's
46:31:44 specific to the loggedin user. And so,
46:31:46 if the answer to this is no, then you
46:31:49 would end up using dynamic serverside
46:31:51 rendering or SSR. In the case of dynamic
46:31:54 serverside rendering, generally
46:31:55 speaking, you will never display stale
46:31:57 information to people accessing that
46:31:59 page. Every time the request is made,
46:32:01 the server will immediately on demand
46:32:04 build the new HTML and send it back. In
46:32:06 the process of building that HTML, it
46:32:08 will call out to an API to get updated
46:32:10 information or speak to a database to
46:32:12 get the most updated information from
46:32:14 the database. Use it to populate the
46:32:16 fields inside the HTML and serve that
46:32:18 back to the client. This means that
46:32:20 every time the page gets refreshed, a
46:32:22 new version of that page, even if the
46:32:24 information looks the same, will get
46:32:26 served to the client. All right, let's
46:32:27 talk about this asterisk for a second.
46:32:29 The nice thing about Nex.js is it will
46:32:32 figure out which rendering strategy to
46:32:33 use for you. There are certain things in
46:32:35 the code that you can trigger, and
46:32:37 that's what we're mostly going to be
46:32:38 spending our time with that will allow

47:22:25 know, I just looked this up and the form
47:22:27 element became an official part of the
47:22:29 HTML language in 1995 and that happened
47:22:32 when forms were standardized in the HTML
47:22:35 2.0 specification. We talked about how
47:22:37 submitting a form is actually performing
47:22:39 a navigation on the website and that
47:22:42 navigation is happening as a full
47:22:44 browser navigation. The entire page is
47:22:46 reloading from scratch. The truth is
47:22:49 however and you know this because of the
47:22:50 link element that you were introduced to
47:22:52 in section one. Nex.js is performing
47:22:55 clientside navigation logic under the
47:22:58 hood which means from one route to
47:23:00 another we can maintain things like
47:23:02 react state without losing it. and it
47:23:05 just makes the whole process a lot
47:23:07 faster when we navigate from one route
47:23:09 to another. Because of this, Nex.js uses
47:23:11 a form component which is kind of built
47:23:14 on top of the native form HTML element.
47:23:18 And using it is just about as simple as
47:23:20 it gets. I need to first import capital
47:23:22 F from next/form.
47:23:25 This one, by the way, is a lowercase F
47:23:28 in form. The next step is I can simply
47:23:30 replace my lowercase F form with a
47:23:33 capital F form. And lastly, in order to
47:23:36 achieve the clientside navigation that
47:23:38 happens when we submit this form, I need
47:23:40 to include an action prop. And this
47:23:43 actually matches the HTML spec for the
47:23:46 lowercase F form. There is an action
47:23:48 prop that you can use with lowercase F
47:23:50 form, which made it so that when you
47:23:52 submitted the form, it would navigate
47:23:54 you to the route that you specify in the
47:23:56 action. A lot of times that route would
47:23:58 actually be something like a PHP file.
47:24:01 So it would be something like I don't
47:24:03 know home.php
47:24:05 which would send the form data to the
47:24:07 home.php file which could then receive
47:24:10 that form data process it decide where
47:24:12 the user needed to then go. Sometimes it
47:24:15 would send them back to the current
47:24:16 page. For us however we are going to
47:24:19 indicate what route we want next.js to
47:24:21 send the user to. In our case, because
47:24:24 we're not sending them to a different
47:24:25 page, we can simply put slash here
47:24:28 because this catfax site where the

47:24:36 slashcatfax or whatever, then I would
47:24:38 want to do the same thing here. I just
47:24:40 want to make sure that whatever I put
47:24:42 here in the action matches the current
47:24:45 location of where I am submitting this
47:24:47 form, at least in my case, because I
47:24:49 don't want to navigate to a different

47:27:01 and celebrate these little wins that we
47:27:03 get with using this capital F form. Now,
47:27:06 one thing I didn't mention is this form
47:27:08 component uses something called
47:27:09 progressive enhancement, which means
47:27:11 that if the user were ever to find
47:27:12 themselves without access to JavaScript,
47:27:15 either the JavaScript failed to load for
47:27:17 whatever reason, maybe they have a
47:27:19 really slow internet connection and they
47:27:21 are trying to interact with the page
47:27:23 before JavaScript has finished loading,
47:27:25 or maybe even they have a browser
47:27:27 extension or browser setting that has
47:27:29 turned off JavaScript altogether for
47:27:31 security or privacy concerns. This
47:27:33 capital F form will automatically
47:27:36 downgrade itself to the native HTML form
47:27:39 element and it will act exactly the way
47:27:41 that it was before we made this upgrade.
47:27:43 It's called progressive enhancement
47:27:45 because we offer a path for the app to
47:27:47 use an enhanced version using capital F
47:27:50 form with clientside navigation, but it
47:27:52 also provides a fallback which will make
47:27:54 it so that your app doesn't completely
47:27:56 break if something were to go wrong when
47:27:58 loading this JavaScript. All right,
